By working closely with subject matter experts, we curate a set of claims from real materials science papers to test whether coding agents can recover and execute the end-to-end workflow needed to support (or undermine) such claims. We then evaluate multiple representative coding agent settings across several foundation models. Our results show that current LLM-based agents obtain low overall success rates on AutoMat, with the best-performing setting achieving a success rate of only 54.1%. Error analysis further reveals that agents perform worst when workflows must be reconstructed from paper text alone and that they fail primarily due to incomplete procedures, methodological deviations, and execution fragility. Taken together, these findings position AutoMat as both a benchmark for computational scientific reproducibility and a tool for diagnosing the current limitations of agentic systems in AI-for-science settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AutoMat, a benchmark designed to evaluate large language models (LLMs) in reproducing scientific claims from computational materials science workflows, highlighting their current limitations in this domain.
AI.motivation: To assess the effectiveness of LLMs as coding agents in the complex domain of computational science, overcoming challenges beyond coding skills.
AI.method: AutoMat benchmark involves three challenges: recovering computational procedures, navigating specialized toolchains, and evaluating evidence supporting scientific claims, using curated claims from real materials science papers.
AI.result: Current LLM-based agents achieved low success rates on AutoMat, with the highest at 54.1%, particularly struggling with reconstruction of workflows from paper text.
AI.conclusion: AutoMat serves as a benchmark for reproducibility in computational science and a tool for identifying the limitations of AI agents in scientific workflows.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning for Chartered Accountancy
summary: The inception of Large Language Models (LLMs) has catalyzed AI adoption in the finance sector, yet their reliability in complex, jurisdiction-specific tasks like Indian Chartered Accountancy (CA) remains limited. The models display difficulty in executing numerical tasks which require multiple steps while also needing advanced knowledge about legal regulations and the method of scaling their operations is not feasible in settings which have limited access to resources. We present CA-ThinkFlow as a parameter-efficient Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework which operates with a 14B, 4-bit-quantized reasoning model, 14B-DeepSeek-R1, and a layout-aware Docling extraction system which maintains document structure during extraction. CA-ThinkFlow uses a basic RAG method which automatically adds retrieved information into the prompt, while it depends on the model's built-in Chain-of-Thought (CoT) functions to create context and produce correct answers. The system we developed system operates at performance levels which match large proprietary models when we tested it on the multi-level CA-Ben benchmark, achieving Scholastic Reliability Coefficient (SRC) results which equal 68.75\% of GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet. The framework shows high efficiency and strength in handling parameters, but essential reasoning abilities fail to process complex regulatory texts which exist in fields such as Taxation.

AI.tldr: CA-ThinkFlow is introduced as a parameter-efficient RAG framework for enhancing AI's effectiveness in complex tasks like Indian Chartered Accountancy, addressing limitations of existing LLMs.
AI.motivation: Despite the promise of LLMs in finance, their reliability for jurisdiction-specific tasks, especially in complex regulatory contexts, remains inadequate.
AI.method: CA-ThinkFlow utilizes a 14B parameter, 4-bit-quantized reasoning model and a layout-aware extraction system, employing a basic RAG approach with Chain-of-Thought functionalities for improved context generation.
AI.result: The CA-ThinkFlow framework matches performance levels of large proprietary models on the CA-Ben benchmark, achieving a Scholastic Reliability Coefficient of 68.75% compared to GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet.
AI.conclusion: While CA-ThinkFlow excels in parameter efficiency, it struggles with complex regulatory texts, indicating a need for further improvement in reasoning capabilities.

title: Learning while Deploying: Fleet-Scale Reinforcement Learning for Generalist Robot Policies
summary: Generalist robot policies increasingly benefit from large-scale pretraining, but offline data alone is insufficient for robust real-world deployment. Deployed robots encounter distribution shifts, long-tail failures, task variations, and human correction opportunities that fixed demonstration datasets cannot fully capture. We present Learning While Deploying (LWD), a fleet-scale offline-to-online reinforcement learning framework for continual post-training of generalist Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies. Starting from a pretrained VLA policy, LWD closes the loop between deployment, shared physical experience, policy improvement, and redeployment by using autonomous rollouts and human interventions collected across a robot fleet. To stabilize learning from heterogeneous, sparse-reward fleet data, LWD combines Distributional Implicit Value Learning (DIVL) for robust value estimation with Q-learning via Adjoint Matching (QAM) for policy extraction in flow-based VLA action generators. We validate LWD on a fleet of 16 dual-arm robots across eight real-world manipulation tasks, including semantic grocery restocking and 3--5 minute long-horizon tasks. A single generalist policy improves as fleet experience accumulates, reaching an average success rate of 95%, with the largest gains on long-horizon tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Learning While Deploying (LWD), a reinforcement learning framework that allows generalist robot policies to improve post-deployment through shared experiences and human interventions, demonstrating strong performance in real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this research is to enhance the robustness and adaptability of generalist robot policies through continual learning, addressing the limitations of fixed offline datasets in real-world scenarios where distribution shifts and task variations occur.
AI.method: The approach utilizes a fleet-scale offline-to-online reinforcement learning framework, incorporating Distributional Implicit Value Learning (DIVL) for value estimation and Q-learning via Adjoint Matching (QAM) for policy extraction, while leveraging human interventions and autonomous rollouts for continual learning.
AI.result: LWD was validated on 16 dual-arm robots performing eight real-world manipulation tasks, achieving an average success rate of 95% as fleet experience increased, with significant improvements noted on long-horizon tasks.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate that pre-trained generalist policies can be effectively improved through continual post-training in a real-world setting, achieving high success rates and adaptability in performing complex tasks.

title: Efficient Spatio-Temporal Vegetation Pixel Classification with Vision Transformers
summary: Plant phenology-the study of recurrent life cycle events-is essential for understanding ecosystem dynamics and their responses to climate change impacts. While Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) and near-surface cameras enable high-resolution monitoring, identifying plant species across time remains computationally challenging. State-of-the-art approaches, specifically Multi-Temporal Convolutional Networks (CNNs), rely on rigid multi-branch architectures that scale poorly with longer time series and require large spatial context windows. In this paper, we present an extensive study on optimizing Vision Transformers (ViTs) for efficient spatio-temporal vegetation pixel classification. We conducted a comprehensive ablation study analyzing seven key design dimensions, including: (i) data normalization; (ii) spectral arrangement; (iii) boundary handling; (iv) spatial context window shape and size; (v) tokenization strategies; (vi) positional encoding; and (vii) feature aggregation strategies. Our method was evaluated on two datasets from the Brazilian Cerrado biome, Serra do Cipó (aerial imagery) and Itirapina (near-surface imagery). Experimental results demonstrate that our ViT approach offers a substantial improvement in computational efficiency while maintaining competitive classification performance. Notably, our ViT reduces Floating Point Operations (FLOPs) by an order of magnitude and maintains constant parameter complexity regardless of the time series length, whereas the CNN baseline scales linearly. Our findings confirm that ViTs are a robust, scalable solution for resource-constrained phenological monitoring systems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an optimized approach using Vision Transformers for efficient spatio-temporal vegetation pixel classification, demonstrating improved computational efficiency over traditional CNNs in phenological monitoring.
AI.motivation: Understanding plant phenology is crucial for ecosystem dynamics and climate response but traditional methods struggle with computational challenges in species identification over time.
AI.method: The study explores seven design dimensions to optimize Vision Transformers for spatio-temporal classification and conducts evaluations on two datasets from the Brazilian Cerrado biome.
AI.result: The ViT method achieved significant improvements in computational efficiency, reducing FLOPs by an order of magnitude while keeping classification performance competitive.
AI.conclusion: Vision Transformers provide a scalable and efficient alternative for phenological monitoring in resource-constrained settings.

title: Semia: Auditing Agent Skills via Constraint-Guided Representation Synthesis
summary:
An agent skill is a configuration package that equips an LLM-driven agent with a concrete capability, such as reading email, executing shell commands, or signing blockchain transactions. Each skill is a hybrid artifact-a structured half declares executable interfaces, while a prose half dictates when and how those interfaces fire-and the prose is reinterpreted probabilistically on every invocation. Conventional static analyzers parse the structured half but ignore the prose; LLM-based tools read the prose but cannot reproducibly prove that a tainted input reaches a high-impact sink.
  We present Semia, a static auditor for agent skills. Semia lifts each skill into the Skill Description Language (SDL), a Datalog fact base that captures LLM-triggered actions, prose-defined conditions, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints. Synthesizing a fact base that is both structurally sound and semantically faithful to the original prose is the central challenge; we address it with Constraint-Guided Representation Synthesis (CGRS), a propose-verify-evaluate loop that refines LLM candidates until convergence. Security properties (e.g., indirect injection, secret leakage, confused deputies, unguarded sinks, etc.) over an agent skill can then be reduced to Datalog reachability queries. We evaluate Semia on 13,728 real-world skills from public marketplaces. Semia renders all of them auditable and finds that more than half carry at least one critical semantic risk. On a stratified sample of 541 expert-labeled skills, Semia achieves 97.7% recall and an F1 of 90.6%, substantially outperforming signature-based scanners and LLM baselines.

AI.tldr: Semia is a static auditor for agent skills, synthesizing a structured Datalog fact base to assess security risks and outperforms existing tools in risk detection.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of conventional static analyzers and LLM-based tools in auditing agent skills that combine prose with executable interfaces.
AI.method: Semia lifts skills into a Datalog fact base using Constraint-Guided Representation Synthesis (CGRS) to refine LLM outputs while ensuring structural and semantic integrity.
AI.result: Evaluated on 13,728 skills, Semia discovered that over half contained critical semantic risks and achieved a recall of 97.7% with an F1 score of 90.6% in identifying expert-labeled risks.
AI.conclusion: Semia significantly improves the auditing process for agent skills, effectively identifying potential security vulnerabilities better than previous methodologies.

title: Stereo Multistage Spatial Attention for Real-Time Mobile Manipulation Under Visual Scale Variation and Disturbances
summary: Robots operating in open, unstructured real-world environments must rely on onboard visual perception while autonomously moving across different locations. Continuous changes in onboard camera viewpoints cause significant visual scale variations in target objects, affecting vision-based motion generation. In this work, we present a stereo multistage spatial attention-based deep predictive learning method for real-time mobile manipulation. The proposed methods extracts task-relevant spatial attention points from stereo images and integrates them with robot states through a hierarchical recurrent architecture for closed-loop action prediction. We evaluate the system on four real-world mobile manipulation tasks using a mobile manipulator, including rigid placement, articulated object manipulation, and deformable object interaction. Experiments under randomized initial positions and visual disturbance conditions demonstrate improved robustness and task success rates compared to representative imitation learning and vision-language-action baselines under identical control settings. The results indicate that structured stereo spatial attention combined with predictive temporal modeling provides an effective solution within the evaluated mobile manipulation scenarios.

AI.tldr: Develops a deep learning method for improving robot motion generation using spatial attention from stereo images for real-time mobile manipulation.
AI.motivation: To enhance the robustness and success rates of robots in unstructured environments with varying visual scales due to camera viewpoint changes.
AI.method: Utilizes a stereo multistage spatial attention approach integrated with a hierarchical recurrent architecture for predicting closed-loop actions from stereo images and robot states.
AI.result: The method outperforms traditional imitation learning and vision-language-action approaches in four real-world manipulation tasks under varying conditions.
AI.conclusion: Structured stereo spatial attention combined with predictive modeling is effective for mobile manipulation in dynamic environments.

title: How Language Models Process Out-of-Distribution Inputs: A Two-Pathway Framework
comment: 30 pages, 3 figures, 30+ tables. Submitted to COLM 2026
summary: Recent white-box OOD detection methods for LLMs -- including CED, RAUQ, and WildGuard confidence scores -- appear effective, but we show they are structurally confounded by sequence length (|r| >= 0.61) and collapse to near-chance under length-matched evaluation. Even raw attention entropy (mean H(alpha) across heads and layers), a natural baseline we include for completeness, shows the same confound. The confound stems from attention's Theta(log T) dependence on input length. To identify genuine OOD signals after deconfounding, we propose a two-pathway framework: embeddings capture what text is about (effective for topic shifts), while the processing trajectory -- hidden-state evolution across layers -- captures how the model processes input. The relative power of each pathway varies along a vocabulary-transparency spectrum: embedding methods excel on vocabulary-distinctive OOD, while trajectory features detect covert-intent inputs that share vocabulary with normal text (0.721 avg AUROC; Jailbreak: 0.850). Three evidence lines support this framework: (1) a crossover between k-NN and trajectory scoring across 6 tasks, where each pathway wins on different OOD types; (2) a per-layer analysis showing that layer-0 k-NN signal is almost entirely a length artifact (Jailbreak: 0.759 raw -> 0.389 matched) -- processing constructs genuine OOD signal from near-chance embeddings; and (3) circuit attribution showing adversarial tasks engage attention circuits more than semantic tasks (p = 0.022; Jailbreak patching p < 0.001), with partial cross-model replication. Code release upon publication.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques existing white-box out-of-distribution (OOD) detection methods for LLMs, revealing their confounding by sequence length. It proposes a new two-pathway framework for effective OOD detection, supported by evidence from various analyses.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the structural confounding of OOD detection methods due to sequence length, which affects their reliability and efficacy in evaluating models.
AI.method: The proposed framework utilizes two pathways: embeddings, which identify content topics, and processing trajectories, which analyze the model's hidden-state evolution across layers, enabling effective detection of OOD signals.
AI.result: The two-pathway approach demonstrates improved OOD detection performance (0.721 avg AUROC; Jailbreak: 0.850), with distinct advantages for different types of OOD inputs based on vocabulary transparency.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes evidences supporting the necessity of deconfounding for accurate OOD detection, and emphasizes the importance of processing trajectories alongside embeddings in this context.

title: Learning Fingerprints for Medical Time Series with Redundancy-Constrained Information Maximization
summary: Learning meaningful representations from medical time series (MedTS) such as ECG or EEG signals is a critical challenge. These signals are often high-dimensional, variable-length and rife with noise. Existing self-supervised approaches, such as Masked Autoencoders (MAEs) are highly effective for pre-training general-purpose encoders. However, they do not explicitly learn compact and semantically interpretable latent representations, typically relying on heuristic aggregation strategies such as global average pooling or a designated [CLS] token. We propose a novel framework that compresses a variable-length MedTS into a fixed-size set of $k$ latent Fingerprint Tokens. Our architecture employs a cross-attention bottleneck to generate these tokens and is trained with a dual-objective function. The first objective is a reconstruction loss, which ensures the tokens are \textit{sufficient statistics} for the original data. The second, a diversity penalty based on the Total Coding Rate (TCR), explicitly minimizes the redundancy between tokens, encouraging them to become statistically \textit{disentangled} representations. We present the theoretical justification for our method, framing it as a novel \textbf{Disentangled Rate-Distortion} problem. This approach produces a low-dimensional, interpretable, and sample-efficient representation, where each token is encouraged to capture an independent factor of variation, paving the way for more robust digital biomarkers.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel framework for compressing medical time series data into interpretable latent representations using Fingerprint Tokens and a dual-objective function for better extraction of meaningful information.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of high-dimensional, variable-length, and noisy medical time series data, while improving the learning of compact and semantically interpretable representations compared to existing methods.
AI.method: The proposed framework compresses variable-length medical time series into a fixed-size set of Fingerprint Tokens using a cross-attention bottleneck and trains it with a dual-objective function: reconstruction loss and diversity penalty based on Total Coding Rate (TCR).
AI.result: The framework enables the generation of low-dimensional, interpretable representations, where tokens encapsulate independent factors of variation, reducing redundancy and enhancing representation quality.
AI.conclusion: The method offers a theoretical framework framed as a Disentangled Rate-Distortion problem, leading to more robust and interpretable digital biomarkers suitable for medical applications.

title: Block-wise Codeword Embedding for Reliable Multi-bit Text Watermarking
summary: Recent multi-bit watermarking methods for large language models (LLMs) prioritize capacity over reliability, often conflating decoding with detection. Our analysis reveals that existing ECC-based extractors suffer from catastrophic false positive rates (FPR), and applying rejection thresholds merely collapses detection sensitivity (TPR) to random guessing. To resolve this structural limitation, we propose \textbf{BREW} (Block-wise Reliable Embedding for Watermarking), a framework shifting the paradigm to \emph{designated verification}. BREW employs a two-stage mechanism: (i) \textbf{blind message estimation} via independent block voting, followed by (ii) \textbf{window-shifting verification} that rigorously validates the payload against local edits. Experiments demonstrate that BREW achieves a TPR of 0.965 with an FPR of 0.02 under 10\% synonym substitution, demonstrating that the high-FPR issue is not an inherent trade-off of multi-bit watermarking, but a solvable structural flaw of prior decoding-centric designs. Our framework is model-agnostic and theoretically grounded, providing a scalable solution for reliable forensic deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BREW, a new framework for multi-bit watermarking in large language models that emphasizes reliability over capacity, overcoming high false positive rates found in existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-bit watermarking methods prioritize capacity, leading to high false positive rates and unreliable detection.
AI.method: The proposed BREW framework utilizes a two-stage mechanism involving blind message estimation through block voting and window-shifting verification to validate payloads against local edits.
AI.result: BREW achieves a true positive rate of 0.965 with a false positive rate of 0.02 under 10% synonym substitution.
AI.conclusion: BREW demonstrates that the issues related to high FPR are structural flaws in previous designs, providing a scalable, model-agnostic solution for reliable watermarking.

title: Beyond Visual Fidelity: Benchmarking Super-Resolution Models for Large-Scale Remote Sensing Imagery via Downstream Task Integration
summary: Super-resolution (SR) techniques have made major advances in reconstructing high-resolution images from low-resolution inputs. The increased resolution provides visual enhancement and utility for monitoring tasks. In particular, SR has been increasingly developed for satellite-based Earth observation, with applications in urban planning, agriculture, ecology, and disaster response. However, existing SR studies and benchmarks typically use fidelity metrics such as PSNR or SSIM, whereas the true utility of super-resolved images lies in supporting downstream tasks such as land cover classification, biomass estimation, and change detection. To bridge this gap, we introduce GeoSR-Bench, a downstream task-integrated SR benchmark dataset to evaluate SR models beyond fidelity metrics. GeoSR-Bench comprises spatially co-located, temporally aligned, and quality-controlled image pairs from about 36,000 locations across diverse land covers, spanning resolutions from 500m to 0.6m. To the best of our knowledge, GeoSR-Bench is the first SR benchmark that directly connects improved image resolution from SR models with downstream Earth monitoring tasks, including land cover segmentation, infrastructure mapping, and biophysical variable estimation. Using GeoSR-Bench, we benchmark GAN, transformer, neural operator, and diffusion-based SR models on perceptual quality and downstream task performance. We conduct experiments with 270 settings, covering 2 cross-platform SR tasks, 9 SR models, 3 downstream task models, and 5 downstream tasks for each SR task. The results show that improvements in traditional SR metrics often do not correlate with gains in task performance, and the correlations can be negative, indicating that these metrics provide limited guidance for selecting superior models for downstream tasks. This reveals the need to integrate downstream tasks into SR model development and evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GeoSR-Bench, a novel benchmark dataset for evaluating super-resolution (SR) models in the context of their utility for downstream Earth monitoring tasks, highlighting that traditional fidelity metrics do not align with performance in these tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between traditional super-resolution evaluation metrics and the actual utility of super-resolved images for downstream tasks such as land cover classification.
AI.method: GeoSR-Bench was created as a benchmark dataset integrating spatially and temporally aligned image pairs from 36,000 locations, covering different land covers and resolutions, to evaluate various SR models and downstream task performances.
AI.result: The experiments benchmarked several SR and downstream task models, revealing that traditional SR metrics like PSNR and SSIM do not correlate with improved performance in practical applications, sometimes showing negative correlations.
AI.conclusion: The findings emphasize the need for incorporating downstream task considerations into the development and evaluation of super-resolution models.

title: Are You the A-hole? A Fair, Multi-Perspective Ethical Reasoning Framework
summary: Standard methods for aggregating natural language judgments, such as majority voting, often fail to produce logically consistent results when applied to high-conflict domains, treating differing opinions as noise. We propose a neuro-symbolic aggregation framework that formalizes conflict resolution through Weighted Maximum Satisfiability (MaxSAT). Our pipeline utilizes a language model to map unstructured natural language explanations into interpretable logical predicates and confidence weights. These components are then encoded as soft constraints within the Z3 solver, transforming the aggregation problem into an optimization task that seeks the maximum consistency across conflicting testimony. Using the Reddit r/AmItheAsshole forum as a case study in large-scale moral disagreement, our system generates logically coherent verdicts that diverge from popularity-based labels 62% of the time, corroborated by an 86% agreement rate with independent human evaluators. This study demonstrates the efficacy of coupling neural semantic extraction with formal solvers to enforce logical soundness and explainability in the aggregation of noisy human reasoning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a neuro-symbolic framework for aggregating conflicting natural language judgments, improving logical consistency in decision-making, particularly in high-conflict domains.
AI.motivation: Standard aggregation methods fail to manage divergent opinions in high-conflict situations, leading to inconsistent outcomes.
AI.method: The proposed framework uses a language model to convert natural language explanations into logical predicates with confidence weights, which are then processed using the Z3 solver to optimize consistency.
AI.result: In a case study of Reddit's r/AmItheAsshole forum, the system provided coherent verdicts that disagreed with popularity-based labels 62% of the time and achieved 86% agreement with human evaluators.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that integrating neural semantic methods with formal solvers enhances logical soundness and explainability in aggregating conflicting human judgments.

title: Structure-Aware Chunking for Tabular Data in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
comment: 5 Pages, 1 figure, 4 Tables, 1 Algorithm, Work In Progress
summary: Tabular documents such as CSV and Excel files are widely used in enterprise data pipelines, yet existing chunking strategies for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) are primarily designed for unstructured text and do not account for tabular structure. We propose a structure-aware tabular chunking (STC) framework that operates on row-level units by constructing a hierarchical Row Tree representation, where each row is encoded as a key-value block. STC performs token-constrained splitting aligned with structural boundaries and applies overlap-free greedy merging to produce dense, non-overlapping chunks. This design preserves semantic relationships between fields within a row while improving token utilization and reducing fragmentation. Across evaluations on the MAUD dataset, STC reduces chunk count by up to 40% and 56% compared to standard recursive and key-value based baselines, respectively, while improving token utilization and processing efficiency. In retrieval benchmarks, STC improves MRR from 0.3576 to 0.5945 in a hybrid setting and increases Recall@1 from 0.366 to 0.754 in BM25-only retrieval. These results demonstrate that preserving structure during chunking improves retrieval performance, highlighting the importance of structure-aware chunking for RAG over tabular data.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a structure-aware tabular chunking (STC) framework that enhances retrieval performance for tabular documents by preserving their structural relationships, resulting in significant improvements in token utilization and efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing chunking strategies for tabular data in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, especially as they are typically designed for unstructured text.
AI.method: The STC framework constructs hierarchical Row Trees for tabular documents, encoding rows as key-value blocks, and performs token-constrained splitting and greedy merging to create dense, non-overlapping chunks.
AI.result: STC reduces chunk count by up to 40% and 56% compared to standard recursive and key-value based methods, respectively. It improves retrieval metrics significantly, including MRR from 0.3576 to 0.5945 and Recall@1 from 0.366 to 0.754.
AI.conclusion: The results underscore the effectiveness of structure-aware chunking in improving retrieval outcomes for RAG systems dealing with tabular data.

title: MSACT: Multistage Spatial Alignment for Stable Low-Latency Fine Manipulation
summary: Real-world fine manipulation, particularly in bimanual manipulation, typically requires low-latency control and stable visual localization, while collecting large-scale data is costly and limited demonstrations may lead to localization drift. Existing approaches make different trade-offs: action-chunking policies such as ACT enable low-latency execution and data efficiency but rely on dense visual features without explicit spatial consistency, generative methods such as Diffusion Policy improve expressiveness but can incur iterative sampling latency, vision-language-action and voxel-based methods enhance generalization and geometric grounding but require higher computational cost and system complexity. We introduce a multistage spatial attention module that extracts stable 2D attention points and jointly predicts future attention sequences with a temporal alignment loss. Built upon ACT with a pretrained ResNet visual prior, a multistage attention module extracts task-relevant 2D attention points as a local spatial modality for action prediction. To maintain consistent object tracking, we introduce a self-supervised objective that aligns predicted attention sequences with visual features from future frames, suppressing drift without keypoint annotations and improving stability of the vision-to-action mapping under limited data. Experiments on simulated and real-world fine manipulation tasks, conducted on the ALOHA bimanual platform, evaluate task success, attention drift, inference latency, and robustness to visual disturbances. Results indicate improvements in localization stability and task performance while maintaining low-latency inference under the tested conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a multistage spatial attention module that enhances bimanual manipulation tasks by improving localization stability and reducing inference latency while using limited data.
AI.motivation: The need for efficient and stable control in fine manipulation tasks, especially bimanual ones, which face challenges such as localization drift due to limited data and high computational costs.
AI.method: A multistage spatial attention module is introduced to predict stable 2D attention points and future attention sequences, along with a self-supervised objective for aligning these predictions with visual features to suppress drift.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate enhanced localization stability, improved task performance, and low-latency inference across simulated and real-world fine manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively improves bimanual manipulation by maintaining consistent object tracking and enhancing visual-to-action mapping, even with limited training data.

title: Online Self-Calibration Against Hallucination in Vision-Language Models
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) often suffer from hallucinations, generating descriptions that include visual details absent from the input image. Recent preference alignment methods typically rely on supervision distilled from stronger models such as GPT. However, this offline paradigm introduces a Supervision-Perception Mismatch: the student model is forced to align with fine-grained details beyond its perceptual capacity, learning to guess rather than to see. To obtain reliable self-supervision for online learning, we identify a Generative-Discriminative Gap within LVLMs, where models exhibit higher accuracy on discriminative verification than open-ended generation. Leveraging this capability, we propose \textbf{O}nline \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{CA}lib\textbf{R}ation (OSCAR), a framework that integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search with a Dual-Granularity Reward Mechanism to construct preference data and iteratively refines the model via Direct Preference Optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OSCAR achieves state-of-the-art performance on hallucination benchmarks while improving general multimodal capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses hallucination issues in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) by proposing OSCAR, an online self-calibration framework that improves model performance by utilizing preference data and optimizing through Direct Preference Optimization.
AI.motivation: To mitigate hallucinations in LVLMs caused by reliance on misaligned supervision from stronger models, which leads to inaccurate detail generation beyond the model's perceptual capacity.
AI.method: OSCAR integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search and a Dual-Granularity Reward Mechanism to generate preference data and employs Direct Preference Optimization for iterative model refinement.
AI.result: OSCAR achieves state-of-the-art performance on hallucination benchmarks and enhances overall multimodal capabilities of the LVLMs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed OSCAR framework offers a sustainable solution for reducing hallucinations in LVLMs while improving their general performance.

title: Smart Profit-Aware Crop Advisory System: Kisan AI
summary: Modern crop advisory systems exhibit a critical limitation termed \textit{economic blindness}. These systems primarily optimize for biological yield, often overlooking market price, which can lead farmers toward agronomically sound yet financially unviable decisions. In this paper, we develop Kisan AI, a smart profit-aware crop advisory system that resolves the above-mentioned limitation through a research-driven, full-stack application. We train the Random Forest(RF) classifier model on a nine-feature benchmark dataset, the standard seven agronomic attributes augmented with a \textit{market\_price} variable, and evaluated against eight baseline models, considering the evaluation matrices, such as, accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Log Loss. The RF model achieves the highest accuracy of 99.3\% and the lowest Log Loss, confirming that the inclusion of market price as a predictive feature is both valid and impactful. We then implement the RF model within a multilingual progressive Web App alongside a Facebook Prophet six-month price forecasting engine and a MobileNetV2 disease detection module. A nine-language AI chatbot powered by the Anthropic Claude API unifies all modules into a single, mobile-installable platform accessible to farmers across India.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Kisan AI, a crop advisory system that integrates market price considerations into agricultural decision-making, using a Random Forest classifier for high predictive accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address the economic blindness in existing crop advisory systems which ignore market prices, leading to unviable farming decisions.
AI.method: Developed Kisan AI, utilizing a Random Forest model trained on a dataset including agronomic features and market price. Evaluated performance using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Log Loss metrics.
AI.result: The Random Forest model achieved 99.3% accuracy and the lowest Log Loss in comparison to eight baseline models, showing the importance of market price in predictions.
AI.conclusion: Kisan AI unifies crop advice, price forecasting, and disease detection in a mobile platform, enhancing accessibility and practical decision-making for farmers.

title: Hierarchical Abstract Tree for Cross-Document Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enhances large language models with external knowledge, and tree-based RAG organizes documents into hierarchical indexes to support queries at multiple granularities. However, existing Tree-RAG methods designed for single-document retrieval face critical challenges in scaling to cross-document multi-hop questions: (1) poor distribution adaptability, where $k$-means clustering introduces noise due to rigid distribution assumptions; (2) structural isolation, as tree indexes lack explicit cross-document connections; and (3) coarse abstraction, which obscures fine-grained details. To address these limitations, we propose $Ψ$-RAG, a tree-RAG framework with two key components. First, a hierarchical abstract tree index built through an iterative "merging and collapse" process that adapts to data distributions without a priori assumption. Second, a multi-granular retrieval agent that intelligently interacts with the knowledge base with reorganized queries and an agent-powered hybrid retriever. $Ψ$-RAG supports diverse tasks from token-level question answering to document-level summarization. On cross-document multi-hop QA benchmarks, it outperforms RAPTOR by 25.9% and HippoRAG 2 by 7.4% in average F1 score. Code is available at https://github.com/Newiz430/Psi-RAG.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces $Ψ$-RAG, an advanced tree-RAG framework addressing limitations of existing multi-hop retrieval systems by using a flexible hierarchical index and a multi-granular retrieval agent, significantly outperforming existing methods in QA tasks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenges faced by current Tree-RAG methods in handling cross-document multi-hop questions, which include poor adaptability, structural isolation, and coarse abstraction.
AI.method: The proposed $Ψ$-RAG framework utilizes a flexible hierarchical abstract tree index that adapts to data distributions through an iterative merging process, and employs a multi-granular retrieval agent for improved query interaction with the knowledge base.
AI.result: $Ψ$-RAG demonstrates a 25.9% improvement over RAPTOR and a 7.4% improvement over HippoRAG 2 on cross-document multi-hop QA benchmarks, as measured by average F1 score.
AI.conclusion: The $Ψ$-RAG framework significantly enhances retrieval capabilities in multi-hop QA tasks and provides code for replication and further research.

title: What Don't You Understand? Using Large Language Models to Identify and Characterize Student Misconceptions About Challenging Topics
summary: This study presents a systematic approach to identifying and characterizing student misconceptions in online learning environments through a novel combination of quantitative performance analysis and large language model (LLM) assessment. We analyzed data from 9 course periods across 5 online biomedical science courses, encompassing 3,802 medical student enrollments. Using data from 40-50 topic-focused quizzes per course, we developed a two-stage methodology. First, we identified challenging central topics using quiz-level performance metrics. Second, we employed LLMs to characterize the underlying misconceptions in these high-priority areas. By examining student performance on first attempts across primarily multiple-choice questions (MCQs), we identified consistently challenging topics that were also central to course objectives. We then leveraged recent advances in generative AI to analyze three distinct data sources in combination: quiz question content, student response patterns, and lecture transcripts. This approach revealed actionable insights about student misconceptions that were not apparent from performance data alone. The quality of the LLM-identified misconceptions was rated as excellent by subject matter experts. We also conducted teacher interviews to assess the perceived utility of our topic identification method. Faculty found that data-driven identification of challenging topics was valuable and corroborated their own classroom observations. This methodology provides a scalable approach to characterizing student difficulties in learning environments where quizzes are used. Our findings demonstrate the potential for targeted and potentially personalized interventions in future course iterations, with clear pathways for measuring intervention effectiveness through follow-up quiz performance.

AI.tldr: The study uses a novel methodology combining quantitative performance analysis and large language models (LLMs) to identify and characterize student misconceptions in online biomedical science courses, revealing actionable insights for improved learning outcomes.
AI.motivation: To address and understand student misconceptions in online learning environments, particularly within biomedical science courses, which can hinder educational achievement.
AI.method: The study employed a two-stage methodology: first, identifying challenging topics using quiz performance metrics, and second, utilizing LLMs to analyze quiz content, student responses, and lecture transcripts for deeper insight into misconceptions.
AI.result: The methodology identified consistently challenging topics and provided high-quality insights into student misconceptions, as rated by subject matter experts. Faculty interviews confirmed the value of data-driven topic identification alongside their classroom observations.
AI.conclusion: This scalable approach enables targeted interventions for addressing student difficulties in online learning, with a framework for measuring effectiveness in subsequent course iterations.

title: Technical Report: Activation Residual Hessian Quantization (ARHQ) for Low-Bit LLM Quantization
summary: We present Activation Residual Hessian Quantization (ARHQ), a post-training weight splitting method designed to mitigate error propagation in low-bit activation-weight quantization. By constructing an input-side residual Hessian from activation quantization residuals (G_x), ARHQ analytically identifies and isolates error-sensitive weight directions into a high-precision low-rank branch. This is achieved via a closed-form truncated SVD on the scaled weight matrix W G^{1/2}_x . Experimental results on Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 demonstrate that ARHQ significantly improves layer-wise SNR and preserves downstream reasoning performance on ZebraLogic even under aggressive quantization. The code is available at https://github.com/BeautMoonQ/ARHQ.

AI.tldr: ARHQ is a method that improves low-bit quantization by addressing error propagation through weight splitting and residual Hessian analysis.
AI.motivation: The method aims to counteract the negative effects of quantization on neural network performance, especially under aggressive low-bit scenarios.
AI.method: ARHQ constructs a residual Hessian from activation quantization residuals and uses truncated SVD to identify and isolate error-sensitive weight directions, splitting them into a high-precision low-rank branch.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that ARHQ enhances layer-wise signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and maintains downstream performance on the ZebraLogic task, even with strong quantization applied.
AI.conclusion: ARHQ effectively mitigates error propagation in quantized networks, allowing for better model performance in low-bit scenarios.

title: Recovering Hidden Reward in Diffusion-Based Policies
summary: This paper introduces EnergyFlow, a framework that unifies generative action modeling with inverse reinforcement learning by parameterizing a scalar energy function whose gradient is the denoising field. We establish that under maximum-entropy optimality, the score function learned via denoising score matching recovers the gradient of the expert's soft Q-function, enabling reward extraction without adversarial training. Formally, we prove that constraining the learned field to be conservative reduces hypothesis complexity and tightens out-of-distribution generalization bounds. We further characterize the identifiability of recovered rewards and bound how score estimation errors propagate to action preferences. Empirically, EnergyFlow achieves state-of-the-art imitation performance on various manipulation tasks while providing an effective reward signal for downstream reinforcement learning that outperforms both adversarial IRL methods and likelihood-based alternatives. These results show that the structural constraints required for valid reward extraction simultaneously serve as beneficial inductive biases for policy generalization. The code is available at https://github.com/sotaagi/EnergyFlow.

AI.tldr: EnergyFlow unifies generative action modeling and inverse reinforcement learning using a scalar energy function gradient, proving that this approach outperforms existing methods in imitation learning and reward extraction.
AI.motivation: To improve the efficiency of reward extraction in reinforcement learning by integrating denoising score matching with generative action modeling.
AI.method: Introduced the EnergyFlow framework which parameterizes a scalar energy function as the denoising field, leveraging concepts like maximum-entropy optimality to recover expert's Q-function gradient without adversarial training.
AI.result: EnergyFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance on various manipulation tasks and provides effective reward signals for downstream tasks, outpacing adversarial IRL methods and likelihood-based strategies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed structural constraints in EnergyFlow not only facilitate valid reward extraction but also enhance policy generalization performance.

title: Pose-Aware Diffusion for 3D Generation
summary: Generating pose-aligned 3D objects is challenging due to the spatial mismatches and transformation ambiguities inherent in decoupled canonical-then-rotate paradigms. To this end, we introduce Pose-Aware Diffusion (PAD), a novel end-to-end diffusion framework that synthesizes 3D geometry directly within the observation space. By unprojecting monocular depth into a partial point cloud and explicitly injecting it as a 3D geometric anchor, PAD abandons canonical assumptions to enforce rigorous spatial supervision. This native generation intrinsically resolves pose ambiguity, producing high-fidelity pose-aligned assets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PAD achieves superior geometric alignment and image-to-3D correspondence compared to state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, PAD naturally extends to compositional 3D scene reconstruction via a simple union of independently generated objects, highlighting its robust ability to preserve precise spatial layouts.

AI.tldr: Pose-Aware Diffusion (PAD) is a new framework that directly synthesizes pose-aligned 3D objects, enhancing spatial accuracy and resolving pose ambiguity compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses challenges in generating pose-aligned 3D objects caused by spatial mismatches and transformation ambiguities in traditional approaches.
AI.method: PAD introduces an end-to-end diffusion framework that uses monocular depth to create a partial point cloud, integrating it as a 3D geometric anchor to ensure spatial accuracy.
AI.result: PAD outperforms state-of-the-art methods in geometric alignment and image-to-3D correspondence through rigorous spatial supervision.
AI.conclusion: The framework not only produces high-fidelity pose-aligned 3D assets but also supports compositional 3D scene reconstruction by maintaining accurate spatial layouts.

title: CleanBase: Detecting Malicious Documents in RAG Knowledge Databases
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is vulnerable to prompt injection attacks, in which an adversary inserts malicious documents containing carefully crafted injected prompts into the knowledge database. When a user issues a question targeted by the attack, the RAG system may retrieve these malicious documents, whose injected prompts mislead it into generating attacker-specified answers, thereby compromising the integrity of the RAG system. In this work, we propose CleanBase, a method to detect malicious documents within a knowledge database. Our key insight is that malicious documents crafted for the same attack-targeted questions often exhibit high semantic similarity, as attackers deliberately make them consistent to improve attack success rates. Accordingly, CleanBase constructs a similarity graph over the knowledge database, where each node represents a document and an edge connects two nodes if their semantic similarity--computed using an embedding model--exceeds a statistically determined threshold. Due to their inherent similarity, malicious documents tend to form cliques within this graph. CleanBase detects such cliques and flags the corresponding documents as malicious. We theoretically derive upper bounds on CleanBase's false positive and false negative rates and empirically validate its effectiveness. Experimental results across multiple datasets and prompt injection attacks demonstrate that CleanBase accurately detects malicious documents and effectively safeguards RAG systems. Our source code is available at https://github.com/WeifeiJin/CleanBase.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CleanBase, a method to detect malicious documents in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, which are vulnerable to prompt injection attacks. CleanBase identifies cliques of semantically similar malicious documents using a similarity graph to enhance system integrity.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this work is to address the vulnerability of RAG systems to prompt injection attacks that can lead to the generation of misleading information by compromising the integrity of the knowledge database.
AI.method: CleanBase constructs a similarity graph of the knowledge database, where documents are nodes and edges represent their semantic similarity, identifying cliques of similar documents that are likely to be malicious.
AI.result: CleanBase effectively detects malicious documents while maintaining theoretically derived low false positive and false negative rates, validated through empirical testing on various datasets and attack scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method enhances the security of RAG systems by accurately identifying and flagging malicious documents, thereby protecting data integrity.

title: H-RAG at SemEval-2026 Task 8: Hierarchical Parent-Child Retrieval for Multi-Turn RAG Conversations
summary: We present H-RAG, our submission to SemEval-2026 Task 8 (MTRAGEval), addressing both Task A (Retrieval) and Task C (Generation with Retrieved Passages). Task A evaluates standalone retrieval quality, while Task C assesses end-to-end retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) in multi-turn conversational settings, requiring both accurate answer generation and faithful grounding in retrieved evidence. Our approach implements a hierarchical parent-child RAG pipeline that separates fine-grained child-level retrieval from parent-level context reconstruction during generation. Documents are segmented into overlapping sentence-based child chunks, while full documents are preserved as parent units to provide coherent context. Retrieval combines hybrid dense-sparse search, tunable weighting, and embedding-based similarity rescoring over child chunks. Retrieved evidence is aggregated at the parent level and supplied to an instruction-tuned language model for response generation. H-RAG achieves an nDCG@5 score of 0.4271 on Task A and a harmonic mean score of 0.3241 on Task C (RB_agg: 0.2488, RL_F: 0.2703, RB_llm: 0.6508), underscoring the importance of retrieval configuration and parent-level aggregation in multi-turn RAG performance.

AI.tldr: H-RAG is designed for SemEval-2026's Task 8, improving retrieval and generation in multi-turn conversations by utilizing a hierarchical RAG pipeline.
AI.motivation: To enhance retrieval quality and generation accuracy in conversational AI by creating a structured approach to combine retrieval and generation effectively.
AI.method: The model features a parent-child hierarchical RAG pipeline with overlapping sentence-based child chunks for fine retrieval, and full documents as context for coherent answer generation, using hybrid dense-sparse search and embedding-based similarity rescoring.
AI.result: H-RAG achieved an nDCG@5 score of 0.4271 on Task A and a harmonic mean score of 0.3241 on Task C, indicating effective retrieval-augmented performance in conversation-based tasks.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight the significance of retrieval setup and parent-level evidence aggregation in optimizing performance for multi-turn conversational generation.

title: Paired-CSLiDAR: Height-Stratified Registration for Cross-Source Aerial-Ground LiDAR Pose Refinement
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures. Dataset and code are being prepared for public release
summary: We introduce Paired-CSLiDAR (CSLiDAR), a cross-source aerial-ground LiDAR benchmark for single-scan pose refinement: refining a ground-scan pose within a 50 m-radius aerial crop. The benchmark contains 12,683 ground-aerial pairs across 6 evaluation sites and per-scan reference 6-DoF alignments for sub-meter root-mean-square error (RMSE) evaluation. Because aerial scans capture rooftops and canopy while ground scans capture facades and under-canopy, the two modalities share only a fraction of their geometry, primarily the terrain surface, causing standard registration methods and learned correspondence models to converge to metrically incorrect local minima. We propose Residual-Guided Stratified Registration (RGSR), a training-free, geometry-only refinement pipeline that exploits the shared ground plane through height-stratified ICP, reversed registration directions, and confidence-gated accept-if-better selection. RGSR achieves 86.0% S@0.75 m and 99.8% S@1.0 m on the primary benchmark of 9,012 scans, outperforming both the confidence-gated cascade at 83.7% and GeoTransformer at 76.3%. We validate RMSE-based pose selection with independent survey control and trajectory consistency, and show that added Fourier-Mellin BEV proposals can reduce RMSE while increasing actual pose error under extreme partial overlap. The dataset and code are being prepared for public release.

AI.tldr: CSLiDAR introduces a benchmark for refining pose estimates of ground scans using aerial LiDAR data, presenting a novel registration method and achieving significant performance improvements.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges in refining ground-scan poses due to inconsistent geometric information between aerial and ground scans, particularly in local minima convergence of standard methods.
AI.method: The proposed method, Residual-Guided Stratified Registration (RGSR), utilizes height-stratified ICP, reversed registration directions, and a confidence-gated accept-if-better selection approach.
AI.result: RGSR demonstrated an S@0.75 m accuracy of 86.0% and S@1.0 m accuracy of 99.8%, outperforming previous methods like the confidence-gated cascade and GeoTransformer.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark and the RGSR method showcase significant advancements in pose refinement and will be available to the public for further research.

title: Wasserstein Distributionally Robust Regret Optimization for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has become a core post-training step for aligning large language models, yet the reward signal used in RLHF is only a learned proxy for true human utility. From an operations research perspective, this creates a decision problem under objective misspecification: the policy is optimized against an estimated reward, while deployment performance is determined by an unobserved objective. The resulting gap leads to reward over-optimization, or Goodharting, where proxy reward continues to improve even after true quality deteriorates. Existing mitigations address this problem through uncertainty penalties, pessimistic rewards, or conservative constraints, but they can be computationally burdensome and overly pessimistic. We propose Wasserstein distributionally robust regret optimization (DRRO) for RLHF. Instead of pessimizing worst-case value as in standard DRO, DRRO pessimizes worst-case regret relative to the best policy under the same plausible reward perturbation. We study the promptwise problem through a simplex allocation model and show that, under an $\ell_1$ ambiguity set, the inner worst-case regret admits an exact solution and the optimal policy has a water-filling structure. These results lead to a practical policy-gradient algorithm with a simple sampled-bonus interpretation and only minor changes to PPO/GRPO-style RLHF training. The framework also clarifies theoretically why DRRO is less pessimistic than DRO, and our experiments show that DRRO mitigates over-optimization more effectively than existing baselines while standard DRO is systematically over-pessimistic.

AI.tldr: This paper presents Wasserstein distributionally robust regret optimization (DRRO) as a solution to mitigate reward over-optimization in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), highlighting its effectiveness compared to traditional approaches.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between learned proxy rewards and true human utility in RLHF, which leads to over-optimization and declines in quality.
AI.method: The authors propose a new framework (DRRO) that focuses on minimizing worst-case regret instead of worst-case value, using a simplex allocation model and $	ext{l}_1$ ambiguity set, leading to a practical policy-gradient algorithm with minor adjustments.
AI.result: DRRO produces less pessimistic results than traditional distributionally robust optimization (DRO) and more effectively mitigates over-optimization in RLHF, as evidenced by experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DRRO framework not only offers practical benefits in RLHF training but also provides theoretical clarity on its advantages over existing methods.

title: Zero-Knowledge Model Checking
summary: We introduce a technology to formally verify that a software system satisfies a temporal specification of functional correctness, without revealing the system itself. Our method combines a deductive approach to model checking to obtain a formal certificate of correctness for the system, with zero-knowledge proofs to convince an external verifier that the system -- kept secret -- complies with its specification of correctness -- made public. We consider proof certificates represented as ranking functions, and introduce both an explicit-state and a symbolic scheme for model checking in zero knowledge. Our explicit-state scheme assumes systems represented as transition graphs. We use polynomial commitments to convince the verifier that the public proof certificates correspond to the secret transition relation. Our symbolic scheme assumes systems specified as linear guarded commands and uses piecewise-linear ranking functions. We apply Farkas' lemma to obtain a witness for the validity of the ranking function with public and secret components, and employ sigma protocols for matrix multiplication and range proofs to convince the verifier of the witness's existence. We built a prototype to demonstrate the practical efficacy of our two schemes on linear temporal logic verification examples. Our technology enables formal verification in domains where both the safety and the confidentiality of the system under analysis are critical.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method for verifying software systems against temporal specifications of correctness while keeping the system confidential, employing a combination of model checking and zero-knowledge proofs.
AI.motivation: To allow for formal verification of software correctness without exposing the system, addressing the need for both safety and confidentiality in critical domains.
AI.method: Combines deductive model checking with zero-knowledge proofs; includes explicit-state and symbolic schemes using polynomial commitments and piecewise-linear ranking functions.
AI.result: Developed a prototype demonstrating practical efficacy in verifying systems via linear temporal logic examples.
AI.conclusion: The proposed technology facilitates confidentiality-preserving formal verification in safety-critical environments.

title: CURE-OOD: Benchmarking Out-of-Distribution Detection for Survival Prediction
summary: ``How long can I live and remain free of cancer?'' is often the first question a patient asks after receiving a cancer diagnosis and treatment. Accurate survival prediction helps alleviate psychological distress and supports risk stratification and personalized treatment planning. Recent survival prediction frameworks have shown strong performance using computed tomography (CT) images. However, variations in imaging acquisition introduce out-of-distribution (OOD) samples caused by covariate shifts that undermine model reliability. Despite this challenge, to our knowledge, no existing benchmark systematically studies OOD detection in cancer survival prediction. To address this gap, we introduce the Cancer sURvival bEnchmark for OOD Detection (CURE-OOD), the first benchmark for systematically evaluating OOD detection in survival prediction under controlled acquisition-induced distribution shifts. CURE-OOD defines scanner-parameter-based training, in-distribution (ID), and OOD test splits across four survival prediction tasks. Our experiments show that covariate shifts notably reduce survival prediction performance. It also shows that mainstream classification-oriented OOD detectors can fail in survival prediction. Finally, we include HazardDev as a simple survival-aware reference baseline for OOD detection. CURE-OOD enables systematic analysis of how distribution shifts affect both downstream survival performance and OOD detectability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CURE-OOD, a benchmark for evaluating out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in cancer survival prediction, addressing the challenges posed by imaging variation.
AI.motivation: Patients seek accurate survival predictions post-cancer diagnosis. Previous frameworks for survival prediction using CT images struggle with variations due to imaging acquisition, leading to unreliable models.
AI.method: CURE-OOD defines controlled training and testing splits based on scanner parameters for four survival prediction tasks. It evaluates the impact of covariate shifts on performance and OOD detection.
AI.result: Experiments reveal that covariate shifts significantly hinder survival prediction performance and that existing OOD detectors, designed for classification, may not be effective in this context.
AI.conclusion: CURE-OOD facilitates systematic examination of the influence of distribution shifts on survival prediction and OOD detection effectiveness.

title: Consistent Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) are an attractive alternative to autoregressive models because they promise sublinear-time, parallel generation, yet practical gains remain elusive as high-quality samples still demand hundreds of refinement steps. In continuous domains, consistency training along the probability-flow ODE is a popular recipe to accelerate diffusion. For discrete diffusion, no analogous sample-space ODE exists, making direct adaptation ill-defined. We argue that the natural discrete substitute is not a deterministic trajectory but its stochastic counterpart: the exact posterior bridge, available in closed form for broad corruption families including masked and uniform diffusion. Building on this observation, we introduce Multi-Path Discrete Consistency (MPDC), a new principle that trains a denoiser to be path-invariant in expectation across these stochastic bridges, and instantiate it as the Consistent Diffusion Language Model (CDLM), a single-stage, teacher-free training framework. A single CDLM objective unifies masked diffusion, continuous consistency models, and progressive/discrete distillation as analytic limits or empirical approximations of one common view. Empirically, CDLM establishes a new state of the art on both conditional and unconditional text-generation, consistently outperforming strong base discrete diffusion models and often even multi-stage distilled baselines across sampling budgets, with the largest gains in the few-step regime. Together, these results position CDLM as a principled and scalable foundation for the next generation of fast, high-fidelity discrete generative modeling.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Consistent Diffusion Language Model (CDLM) which addresses inefficiencies in discrete diffusion language models by using a novel training principle. CDLM achieves state-of-the-art performance in text generation.
AI.motivation: To improve the efficiency and quality of discrete diffusion language models, which currently require many refinement steps for high-quality samples.
AI.method: The paper introduces Multi-Path Discrete Consistency (MPDC), which trains a denoiser to be path-invariant across stochastic bridges, providing a unified training framework for discrete diffusion.
AI.result: CDLM achieves state-of-the-art results in both conditional and unconditional text generation, outperforming existing discrete diffusion models and multi-stage distilled baselines.
AI.conclusion: CDLM establishes a scalable approach for fast, high-fidelity discrete generative modeling.

title: Prompt-Induced Score Variance in Zero-Shot Binary Vision-Language Safety Classification
summary: Single-prompt first-token probabilities from zero-shot vision-language model (VLM) safety classifiers are treated as decision scores, but we show they are unreliable under semantically equivalent prompt reformulation: even when the binary label is constrained to a fixed output position, equivalent prompts can induce materially different unsafe probabilities for the same sample. Across multimodal safety benchmarks and multiple VLM families, cross-prompt variance is strongly associated with prompt-level disagreement and higher error, making it a useful fragility diagnostic. A training-free mean ensemble improves NLL on all 14 dataset-model evaluation pairs and ECE on 12/14 relative to a train-selected single-prompt baseline, and wins more head-to-head NLL comparisons than labeled temperature scaling, Platt scaling, and isotonic regression applied to the same prompt. Ranking gains are consistent against the train-selected baseline on both AUROC and AUPRC, and against the full 15-prompt distribution remain consistent on AUPRC while softening on AUROC. Labeled calibration on top of the mean provides further gains when labels are available, identifying prompt averaging as a strong label-free first stage rather than a replacement for calibration. We frame this as a reliability stress test for zero-shot VLM first-token safety scores and recommend prompt-family evaluation with mean aggregation as a standard label-free reliability baseline.

AI.tldr: This study critiques the reliance on first-token probabilities from zero-shot vision-language models for safety assessments, highlighting their unreliability due to prompt reformulation. It introduces a mean ensemble method that enhances probabilistic evaluations, suggesting it as a reliable non-training-based calibration technique.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to investigate the reliability of first-token probabilities from vision-language model safety classifiers, particularly when faced with semantically equivalent prompt reformulations.
AI.method: The authors analyze cross-prompt variance in safety scores and propose a training-free mean ensemble to mitigate discrepancies while improving metrics such as NLL and ECE across various datasets and models.
AI.result: The mean ensemble method consistently outperforms single-prompt baselines and other calibration techniques in prediction accuracy and reliability measures, showing strong performance across multiple multimodal safety benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The results suggest that prompt-family evaluation with mean aggregation should be adopted as a standard for assessing the reliability of zero-shot VLM safety scores.

title: Affordance Agent Harness: Verification-Gated Skill Orchestration
summary: Affordance grounding requires identifying where and how an agent should interact in open-world scenes, where actionable regions are often small, occluded, reflective, and visually ambiguous. Recent systems therefore combine multiple skills (e.g., detection, segmentation, interaction-imagination), yet most orchestrate them with fixed pipelines that are poorly matched to per-instance difficulty, offer limited targeted recovery from intermediate errors, and fail to reuse experience from recurring objects. These failures expose a systems problem: test-time grounding must acquire the right evidence, decide whether that evidence is reliable enough to commit, and do so under bounded inference cost without access to labels. We propose Affordance Agent Harness, a closed-loop runtime that unifies heterogeneous skills with an evidence store and cost control, retrieves episodic memories to provide priors for recurring categories, and employs a Router to adaptively select and parameterize skills. An affordance-specific Verifier then gates commitments using self-consistency, cross-scale stability, and evidence sufficiency, triggering targeted retries before a final judge fuses accumulated evidence and trajectories into the prediction. Experiments on multiple affordance benchmarks and difficulty-controlled subsets show a stronger accuracy-cost Pareto frontier than fixed-pipeline baselines, improving grounding quality while reducing average skill calls and latency. Project page: https://tenplusgood.github.io/a-harness-page/.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the Affordance Agent Harness, a system designed to enhance affordance grounding in open-world scenes by dynamically coordinating multiple skills and utilizing episodic memory for better decision-making under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve affordance grounding in challenging open-world environments where interactions need to be pinpointed accurately amidst visual ambiguity and occlusions.
AI.method: The proposed method involves a closed-loop runtime that combines various skills, manages an evidence store for contextual information, and employs a Router to flexibly choose and tune skills based on the situation.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate an improved accuracy-cost balance compared to fixed-pipeline approaches, showcasing higher grounding quality while lowering the number of skill calls and processing time.
AI.conclusion: The Affordance Agent Harness effectively addresses the limitations of traditional systems by adapting skill selection and leveraging past experiences, resulting in superior performance in affordance grounding tasks.

title: Time-series Meets Complex Motion Modeling: Robust and Computational-effective Motion Predictor for Multi-object Tracking
summary: Multi-object tracking (MOT) is critical in numerous real-world applications, including surveillance, autonomous driving, and robotics. Accurately predicting object motion is fundamental to MOT, but current methods struggle with the complexities of real-world, non-linear motion (e.g., sudden stops, sharp turns). While recent research has gravitated towards increasingly complex and computationally expensive generative models to tackle this problem, their practical utility is often constrained. This paper challenges that paradigm, arguing that such complexity is not only unnecessary but can be outperformed by a more efficient, purpose-built approach. We introduce the Temporal Convolutional Motion Predictor (TCMP), a novel framework for MOT that leverages a modified Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) featuring dilated convolutions and a regression head. This design allows for effective motion prediction across arbitrary temporal context lengths. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, specifically improves upon the previous best method in several key metrics: HOTA (a measure of overall tracking accuracy) increases from 62.3% to 63.4%, IDF1 (a measure of identity preservation) rises from 63.0% to 65.0%, and AssA (a measure of association accuracy) improves from 47.2% to 49.1%. Significantly, TCMP achieves this performance while being highly efficient; it has only 0.014 times the parameters and requires only 0.05 times the computational cost (FLOPs) compared to the SOTA method. while is only 0.014 times the size (in terms of parameters) and requires only 0.05 times the computational cost (in terms of FLOPs). These findings highlight the robustness of our method to advance MOT systems by ensuring adaptability, accuracy, and efficiency in complex tracking environments.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the Temporal Convolutional Motion Predictor (TCMP), a novel approach to multi-object tracking (MOT) that achieves state-of-the-art performance with significantly lower computational complexity compared to existing generative models.
AI.motivation: Current multi-object tracking methods are limited by their complexity and inefficiency in dealing with non-linear motion patterns in real-world applications.
AI.method: The proposed TCMP utilizes a modified Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) with dilated convolutions and a regression head for effective motion prediction across varying temporal contexts.
AI.result: TCMP improves tracking metrics such as HOTA (from 62.3% to 63.4%), IDF1 (from 63.0% to 65.0%), and AssA (from 47.2% to 49.1%) while being highly efficient, utilizing only 0.014 times the parameters and 0.05 times the computational cost of the state-of-the-art method.
AI.conclusion: The TCMP framework demonstrates that a simpler, more efficient approach can surpass the performance of complex generative models, enhancing adaptability, accuracy, and efficiency in multi-object tracking.

title: Pick and Sort for Graphical Authentication
comment: The paper is accepted for publication at the International Workshop on Software Security 2026 (co-located with EASE 2026)
summary: We propose a graphical authentication scheme that follows a simple ``Pick and Sort'' design in which users choose visual elements and arrange them within a grid. Both the number of selected elements and the grid size are configurable, and the visual elements can be customized for specific user groups, such as children. A preliminary study with a prototype implementation indicated that the scheme is easy to learn and flexible to deploy. Although login times are longer than those of conventional authentication methods, the additional interaction may be acceptable in scenarios that are not time-critical, such as infrequent-access use cases or as a secondary authentication mechanism.

AI.tldr: A new graphical authentication scheme, "Pick and Sort," allows users to select and arrange visual elements in a customizable grid for secure login, showing ease of use despite longer login times.
AI.motivation: To improve authentication methods by offering a user-friendly graphical approach suitable for various demographics, including children.
AI.method: Users select visual elements and organize them in a grid, with configurable options for element number and grid size.
AI.result: Preliminary studies show the scheme is easy to learn and adaptable, though login times are longer than traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: The scheme is viable for non-time-critical situations, providing flexibility as a primary or secondary authentication option.

title: Budget-Aware Routing for Long Clinical Text
summary:
A key challenge for large language models is token cost per query and overall deployment cost. Clinical inputs are long, heterogeneous, and often redundant, while downstream tasks are short and high stakes. We study budgeted context selection, where a subset of document units is chosen under a strict token budget so an off-the-shelf generator can meet fixed cost and latency constraints. We cast this as a knapsack-constrained subset selection problem with two design choices, unitization that defines document segmentation and selection that determines which units are kept.
  We propose \textbf{RCD}, a monotone submodular objective that balances relevance, coverage, and diversity. We compare sentence, section, window, and cluster-based unitization, and introduce a routing heuristic that adapts to the budget regime. Experiments on MIMIC discharge notes, Cochrane abstracts, and L-Eval show that optimal strategies depend on the evaluation setting. Positional heuristics perform best at low budgets in extractive tasks, while diversity-aware methods such as MMR improve LLM generation. Selector choice matters more than unitization, with cluster-based grouping reducing performance and other schemes behaving similarly. ROUGE saturates for LLM summaries, while BERTScore better reflects quality differences. We release our code at https://github.com/stone-technologies/ACL_budget_paper.

AI.tldr: Study of budgeted context selection for large language models to optimize token usage and minimize costs by proposing RCD, a monotone submodular objective.
AI.motivation: Addressing the high costs associated with long and redundant clinical inputs in large language model queries.
AI.method: The knapsack-constrained subset selection problem is formulated to determine document segmentation (unitization) and unit retention (selection), with the introduction of RCD for balancing relevance, coverage, and diversity.
AI.result: Optimal strategies vary by evaluation settings; positional heuristics excel in low-budget extractive tasks while diversity-aware methods enhance LLM generation, with unit selector choice being crucial for performance.
