To this end, we propose Double-Softmax Prompt Tuning (DSPT), a hyperparameter-free method for intrinsic gradient suppression. By applying a sequential probabilistic normalization, DSPT induces a self-adaptive saturation zone that suppresses gradients from high-error noisy samples while maintaining informative updates. We also provide both theoretical analysis and empirical evidence about how this mechanism achieves adaptive suppression. This design transforms ``gradient vanishing'', traditionally a training bottleneck, into a principled noise-filtering shield for label-noise prompt tuning. Extensive experiments confirm that this simple, drop-in design achieves state-of-the-art robustness across various noisy benchmarks, outperforming methods with complex architectures and handcrafted hyperparameters.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Double-Softmax Prompt Tuning (DSPT) to address the sensitivity of prompt tuning in contrastive vision-language models like CLIP to label noise by suppressing high-error gradients while retaining informative updates, resulting in improved robustness.
AI.motivation: Prompt tuning in vision-language models is vulnerable to label noise, which adversely affects model performance due to exaggerated gradient updates.
AI.method: The proposed DSPT uses a hyperparameter-free approach involving sequential probabilistic normalization to create a saturation zone for gradients, enhancing adaptability to noisy labels.
AI.result: DSPT demonstrates effective intrinsic gradient suppression, leading to significant performance improvements on noisy benchmarks, surpassing complex models.
AI.conclusion: DSPT transforms a common training problem into an effective mechanism for noise filtering during prompt tuning, achieving state-of-the-art results in robustness against label noise.

title: VQ-SAD: Vector Quantized Structure Aware Diffusion For Molecule Generation
summary: Many diffusion based molecule generation methods ignore the symbolic information of molecules and represent the atom and bond type as one hot representation. Methods based on Morgan fingerprints produce hash collisions and are hard to embed into a continuous space without information loss and random fingerprints correspond to no valid molecule. To circumvent this issue we use another paradigm and consider atom and bond codes as latent variables of VQ-VAE. We introduce VQ-SAD which first trains a VQ-VAE and uses the frozen pretrained VQ-VAE model and considers the codebooks for both atom and bond types as tokenizers for the downstream diffusion process. VQ-SAD is a neuro-symbolic model that utilizes both symbolic and neural structural information for a diffusion based model with learnable forward process. The large discrete code space provides a more balanced atom and bond types which enhances the denoising process. VQ-VAE slightly outperforms SOTA models for diffusion based molecule generation on QM9 and ZINC250k datasets.

AI.tldr: VQ-SAD improves diffusion-based molecule generation by using a VQ-VAE model to integrate symbolic and neural information, enhancing the denoising process and outperforming SOTA models.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion methods neglect symbolic information, leading to inefficient representations of molecular structures.
AI.method: VQ-SAD employs a VQ-VAE to train on atom and bond codes as latent variables, using frozen codebooks as tokenizers in the diffusion process.
AI.result: VQ-SAD outperforms state-of-the-art models in generating molecules on QM9 and ZINC250k datasets.
AI.conclusion: The integration of discrete code space enhances the balance of atom and bond types, improving the denoising efficiency in molecule generation.

title: Binomial flows: Denoising and flow matching for discrete ordinal data
summary: Flow-based generative modeling in continuous spaces exploit Tweedie's formula to express the denoiser (learned in training) as a score function (used in sampling). In contrast, this relation has been largely missing in the discrete setting where common approaches focus on learning discrete scores and rates. In this work we close this gap for discrete non-negative ordinal data by introducing Binomial flows. Our framework provides a simple recipe for training a discrete diffusion model which simultaneously denoises, samples, and estimates exact likelihoods. We verify our methodology on synthetic examples and obtain competitive results on real-world data sets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Binomial flows for training discrete diffusion models, bridging the gap in score functions for discrete data, with effective results.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a connection between denoising and score functions in discrete generative modeling, particularly for non-negative ordinal data.
AI.method: Introducing a framework called Binomial flows, which allows for simultaneous denoising, sampling, and likelihood estimation of discrete data.
AI.result: The proposed methodology performs competitively on synthetic examples and real-world datasets, demonstrating its efficacy in discrete diffusion modeling.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully establishes a method for training discrete diffusion models that integrates denoising and scoring functions, filling a notable gap in existing research.

title: Learning How and What to Memorize: Cognition-Inspired Two-Stage Optimization for Evolving Memory
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents require long-term user memory for consistent personalization, but limited context windows hinder tracking evolving preferences over long interactions. Existing memory systems mainly rely on static, hand-crafted update rules; although reinforcement learning (RL)-based agents learn memory updates, sparse outcome rewards provide weak supervision, resulting in unstable long-horizon optimization. Drawing on memory schema theory and the functional division between prefrontal regions and hippocampus regions, we introduce MemCoE, a cognition-inspired two-stage optimization framework that learns how memory should be organized and what information to update. In the first stage, we propose Memory Guideline Induction to optimize a global guideline via contrastive feedback interpreted as textual gradients; in the second stage, Guideline-Aligned Memory Policy Optimization uses the induced guideline to define structured process rewards and performs multi-turn RL to learn a guideline-following memory evolution policy. We evaluate on three personalization memory benchmarks, covering explicit/implicit preference and different sizes and noise, and observe consistent improvements over strong baselines with favorable robustness, transferability, and efficiency.

AI.tldr: MemCoE is a two-stage optimization framework for enhancing personalized memory in LLM agents, addressing limitations of existing memory systems.
AI.motivation: Long interactions with LLM agents require effective user memory to capture changing preferences, which current methods struggle with due to static rules and weak RL supervision.
AI.method: The framework comprises two stages: Memory Guideline Induction for optimizing guidelines using contrastive feedback, and Guideline-Aligned Memory Policy Optimization for refining memory policies through multi-turn reinforcement learning.
AI.result: Evaluation on three benchmarks shows that MemCoE outperforms strong baselines in terms of robustness, transferability, and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: MemCoE effectively addresses limitations in user memory for LLMs, enhancing personalization through an innovative cognitive-inspired approach.

title: FinSafetyBench: Evaluating LLM Safety in Real-World Financial Scenarios
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in financial scenarios. However, they may produce harmful outputs, including facilitating illegal activities or unethical behavior, posing serious compliance risks. To systematically evaluate LLM safety in finance, we propose FinSafetyBench, a bilingual (English-Chinese) red-teaming benchmark designed to test an LLM's refusal of requests that violate financial compliance. Grounded in real-world financial crime cases and ethics standards, the benchmark comprises 14 subcategories spanning financial crimes and ethical violations. Through extensive experiments on general-purpose and finance-specialized LLMs under three representative attack settings, we identify critical vulnerabilities that allow adversarial prompts to bypass compliance safeguards. Further analysis reveals stronger susceptibility in Chinese contexts and highlights the limitations of prompt-level defenses against sophisticated or implicit manipulation strategies.

AI.tldr: FinSafetyBench is a new benchmark for testing the safety of LLMs in finance, focusing on their ability to resist compliance violations.
AI.motivation: To address the compliance risks posed by LLMs in financial applications due to harmful outputs and unethical behavior.
AI.method: We developed FinSafetyBench, a bilingual benchmark with 14 subcategories based on real-world financial crimes and ethics, and conducted extensive testing on various LLMs in different attack scenarios.
AI.result: The experiments identified significant vulnerabilities in LLMs, particularly in Chinese contexts, where prompt defenses were often inadequate against manipulation.
AI.conclusion: The study underscores the need for enhanced compliance safeguards in LLMs, especially given their limitations in handling sophisticated prompts.

title: Faithful Extreme Image Rescaling with Learnable Reversible Transformation and Semantic Priors
summary: Most recent extreme rescaling methods struggle to preserve semantically consistent structures and produce realistic details, due to the severely ill-posed nature of low- to high-resolution mapping under scaling factors of $16\times$ or higher. To alleviate the above problems, we propose FaithEIR, a diffusion-based framework for extreme image rescaling. Inspired by singular value decomposition, we develop learnable reversible transformation that enables invertible downscaling and upscaling in the latent space. To compensate for information loss due to quantization, we propose an adaptive detail prior, a high-frequency dictionary that captures the empirical average of commonly occurring structures in the training data. Finally, we design a lightweight pixel semantic embedder to provide semantic conditioning for the pretrained diffusion model. We present extensive experimental results demonstrating that our FaithEIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior reconstruction fidelity and perceptual quality. Our code, model weights, and detailed results are released at https://github.com/cshw2021/FaithEIR.

AI.tldr: FaithEIR is a diffusion-based framework for extreme image rescaling that addresses issues of detail preservation and structure consistency in low- to high-resolution mapping, especially at scaling factors of 16x or higher.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this work is to overcome the challenges posed by extreme rescaling methods that fail to maintain semantic structures and realistic details due to the ill-posed nature of low- to high-resolution mappings.
AI.method: The proposed method involves a learnable reversible transformation inspired by singular value decomposition, enabling invertible downscaling and upscaling in latent space, complemented by an adaptive detail prior for high-frequency structure capture, and a lightweight pixel semantic embedder for semantic conditioning.
AI.result: Experimental results show that FaithEIR outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, leading to improvements in reconstruction fidelity and perceptual quality.
AI.conclusion: FaithEIR provides an effective solution for extreme image rescaling, successfully preserving image quality and semantic details.

title: Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization: Breaking RLVR's Indifference to Diversity
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has achieved substantial gains in single-attempt accuracy (Pass@1) on reasoning tasks, yet often suffers from reduced multi-sample coverage (Pass@K), indicating diversity collapse. We identify a structural cause for this degradation: common RLVR objectives, such as GRPO, are indifferent to how probability mass is distributed among correct solutions. Combined with stochastic training dynamics, this indifference induces a self-reinforcing collapse, in which probability mass concentrates on a narrow subset of correct outputs while alternative valid solutions are suppressed. We formalize this collapse mechanism and further characterize the optimal policy structure under two complementary criteria: robustness and entropy-regularized optimality, which identify the Uniform-Correct Policy as uniquely optimal. Motivated by this analysis, we propose Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization (UCPO), a modification to GRPO that adds a conditional uniformity penalty on the policy's distribution over correct solutions. The penalty redistributes gradient signal toward underrepresented correct responses, encouraging uniform allocation of probability mass within the correct set. Across three models (1.5B-7B parameters) and five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, UCPO improves Pass@K and diversity while maintaining competitive Pass@1, achieving up to +10\% absolute improvement on AIME24 at Pass@64 and up to 45\% higher equation-level diversity within the correct set. The code is available at https://github.com/AnamikaLochab/UCPO.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the issue of diversity collapse in RLVR, proposing UCPO for improved multi-sample coverage while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to tackle the degradation of multi-sample coverage in RLVR, specifically the phenomenon where probability mass concentrates on a narrow set of correct outputs.
AI.method: The authors formalize the collapse mechanism in RLVR and introduce UCPO, which incorporates a conditional uniformity penalty to enhance the distribution of probability mass among valid solutions.
AI.result: Experimental results show that UCPO enhances multi-sample coverage (Pass@K) and diversity, with significant improvements noted in specific benchmarks while keeping Pass@1 competitive.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that UCPO effectively mitigates diversity collapse in RLVR, leading to improved performance in mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: CMTA: Leveraging Cross-Modal Temporal Artifacts for Generalizable AI-Generated Video Detection
summary: The proliferation of advanced AI video synthesis techniques poses an unprecedented challenge to digital video authenticity. Existing AI-generated video (AIGV) detection methods primarily focus on uni-modal or spatiotemporal artifacts, but they overlook the rich cues within the visual-textual cross-modal space, especially the temporal stability of semantic alignment. In this work, we identify a distinctive fingerprint in AIGVs, termed cross-modal temporal artifact (CMTA). Unlike real videos that exhibit natural temporal fluctuations in cross-modal alignment due to semantic variations, AIGVs display unnaturally stable semantic trajectories governed by given input prompts. To bridge this gap, we propose the CMTA framework, a cross-modal detection approach that captures these unique temporal artifacts through joint cross-modal embedding and multi-grained temporal modeling. Specifically, CMTA leverages BLIP to generate frame-level image captions and utilizes CLIP to extract corresponding visual-textual representations. A coarse-grained temporal modeling branch is then designed to characterize temporal fluctuations in cross-modal alignment with a GRU. In parallel, a fine-grained branch is constructed to capture intricate inter-frame variations from integrated visual-textual features with a Transformer encoder. Extensive experiments on 40 subsets across four large-scale datasets, including GenVideo, EvalCrafter, VideoPhy, and VidProM, validate that our approach sets a new state-of-the-art while exhibiting superior cross-generator generalization. Code and models of CMTA will be released at https://github.com/hwang-cs-ime/CMTA

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the CMTA framework for detecting AI-generated videos by identifying cross-modal temporal artifacts, achieving state-of-the-art results in video authenticity detection.
AI.motivation: To address the inadequacies of existing AIGV detection methods that disregard the potential of cross-modal analysis for identifying inconsistencies in AI-generated videos.
AI.method: The CMTA framework employs joint cross-modal embedding and multi-grained temporal modeling, utilizing BLIP for image captioning and CLIP for visual-textual representation extraction while modeling temporal dynamics with GRU and Transformer encoder.
AI.result: The proposed CMTA framework outperforms existing methods on 40 subsets from four large datasets, demonstrating improved detection accuracy and cross-generator generalization.
AI.conclusion: The CMTA framework effectively identifies unique temporal artifacts in AI-generated videos, setting a new benchmark for detection techniques in the field.

title: Characterizing the Expressivity of Local Attention in Transformers
summary: The transformer is the most popular neural architecture for language modeling. The cornerstone of the transformer is its global attention mechanism, which lets the model aggregate information from all preceding tokens before generating the next token. One common variant of attention is called local attention, which restricts each token to aggregating information from a bounded window of predecessors, reducing the quadratic cost of global attention to linear. Although this restriction is usually motivated by efficiency, it has also been found to improve model quality, a phenomenon that has so far lacked a satisfactory explanation. We provide a formal account of this phenomenon in terms of recognizer expressivity. It has been shown that fixed-precision transformers with global attention correspond to a fragment of linear temporal logic containing a single past operator. We additionally prove that adding local attention introduces a second temporal operator, strictly enlarging the class of recognizable regular languages. Moreover, global and local attention are expressively complementary: neither subsumes the other, and combining them yields the richest fragment. Experiments on formal language recognition and natural language modeling corroborate the theory, showing that hybrid global--local transformers outperform their global-only counterparts.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the benefits of local attention in transformers, demonstrating that it not only improves efficiency but also enhances model quality due to increased expressivity in language recognition.
AI.motivation: To explain why local attention, despite being designed for efficiency, improves model quality in transformers, which typically rely on global attention mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors analyze the relationship between transformer architectures and formal language recognition, particularly focusing on the expressivity of fixed-precision transformers with global and local attention.
AI.result: Local attention introduces an additional temporal operator, expanding the class of recognizable languages and demonstrating that global and local attention mechanisms complement each other in expressiveness.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid models that combine global and local attention outperform those using only global attention, confirming the theoretical improvements in language recognition.

title: AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies via Large Language Models with Deployment Guarantees
summary: We study how large language models can be used to evolve inventory policies in online, non-stationary environments. Our work is motivated by recent advances in LLM-based evolutionary search, such as AlphaEvolve, which demonstrates strong performance for static and highly structured problems such as mathematical discovery, but is not directly suited to online dynamic inventory settings. To this end, we propose AlphaInventory, an end-to-end inventory-policy evolution and inference framework grounded in confidence-interval-based certification. The framework trains a large language model using reinforcement learning, incorporates demand data as well as numerical and textual features beyond demand, and generates white-box inventory policy with statistical safety guarantees for deployment in future periods. We further introduce a unified theoretical interface that connects training, inference, and deployment. This allows us to characterize the probability that the AlphaInventory evolves a statistically safe and improved policy, and to quantify the deployment gap relative to the oracle-safe benchmark. Tested on both synthetic data and real-world retail data, AlphaInventory outperforms classical inventory policies and deep learning based methods. In canonical inventory settings, it evolves new policies that improve upon existing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents AlphaInventory, a large language model framework for evolving inventory policies in dynamic environments, showing superior performance over traditional methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the limitations of existing LLM-based evolutionary searches in dynamic inventory management, despite their success in static problems.
AI.method: AlphaInventory employs reinforcement learning to train a large language model, using both demand data and additional features, while ensuring statistical safety through confidence intervals.
AI.result: AlphaInventory significantly outperforms classical inventory policies and deep learning approaches when tested on synthetic and real-world retail data, evolving policies that exceed existing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a systematic way to ensure the statistical safety and improvement of inventory policies in real-world applications.

title: BlenderRAG: High-Fidelity 3D Object Generation via Retrieval-Augmented Code Synthesis
summary: Automatic generation of executable Blender code from natural language remains challenging, with state-of-the-art LLMs producing frequent syntactic errors and geometrically inconsistent objects. We present BlenderRAG, a retrieval-augmented generation system that operates on a curated multimodal dataset of 500 expert-validated examples (text, code, image) across 50 object categories. By retrieving semantically similar examples during generation, BlenderRAG improves compilation success rates from 40.8% to 70.0% and semantic normalized alignment from 0.41 to 0.77 (CLIP similarity) across four state-of-the-art LLMs, without requiring fine-tuning or specialized hardware, making it immediately accessible for deployment. The dataset and code will be available at https://github.com/MaxRondelli/BlenderRAG.

AI.tldr: BlenderRAG is a system that enhances executable Blender code generation from natural language, improving success rates and semantic alignment by retrieving similar examples without the need for fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: The challenge of generating syntactically correct and geometrically consistent Blender code from natural language using current LLMs.
AI.method: BlenderRAG employs a retrieval-augmented generation approach with a multimodal dataset of 500 validated examples to assist in code generation.
AI.result: BlenderRAG increases compilation success rates from 40.8% to 70.0% and improves semantic alignment as measured by CLIP similarity from 0.41 to 0.77.
AI.conclusion: The system provides a significant improvement in code generation performance and is accessible without requiring specialized hardware or fine-tuning.

title: Directed Social Regard: Surfacing Targeted Advocacy, Opposition, Aid, Harms, and Victimization in Online Media
summary: The language in online platforms, influence operations, and political rhetoric frequently directs a mix of pro-social sentiment (e.g., advocacy, helpfulness, compassion) and anti-social sentiment (e.g., threats, opposition, blame) at different topics, all in the same message. While many natural language processing (NLP) tools classify or score a text's overall sentiment as positive, neutral, or negative, these tools cannot report that positive and negative sentiments coexist, and they cannot report the target of those sentiments. This paper presents the Directed Social Regard (DSR) approach to multi-dimensional, multi-valence sentiment analysis, comprised of a pair of transformer-based models that (1) detects span-level targets of sentiment in a message and then (2) scores all spans within the message context along three (-1, 1) axes of regard that are motivated by social science theories of moral disengagement and moral framing. We present a data collection and annotation strategy for DSR dataset construction, a transformer-based architecture for span-level scoring, and a validation study with promising results. We apply the validated DSR model on six third-party datasets of online media and report meaningful correlations between DSR outputs and the labels and topics in these pre-existing social science datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Directed Social Regard (DSR) approach for multi-dimensional sentiment analysis, capable of detecting and scoring both positive and negative sentiments directed at specific targets in online messages.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing NLP tools that fail to capture the coexistence of positive and negative sentiments and their targets in messages.
AI.method: The DSR approach employs two transformer-based models for sentiment detection and scoring across three axes of social regard, developed through a systematic data collection and annotation process.
AI.result: Validation studies showed promising results, indicating that the DSR model effectively measures sentiment across diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: The DSR model correlates meaningfully with existing social science datasets, suggesting its potential for enhanced sentiment analysis in online discourse.

title: UniVidX: A Unified Multimodal Framework for Versatile Video Generation via Diffusion Priors
comment: Project page: https://houyuanchen111.github.io/UniVidX.github.io/ Accepted to ACM Transactions on Graphics (Proceedings of SIGGRAPH 2026)
summary: Recent progress has shown that video diffusion models (VDMs) can be repurposed for diverse multimodal graphics tasks. However, existing methods often train separate models for each problem setting, which fixes the input-output mapping and limits the modeling of correlations across modalities. We present UniVidX, a unified multimodal framework that leverages VDM priors for versatile video generation. UniVidX formulates pixel-aligned tasks as conditional generation in a shared multimodal space, adapts to modality-specific distributions while preserving the backbone's native priors, and promotes cross-modal consistency during synthesis. It is built on three key designs. Stochastic Condition Masking (SCM) randomly partitions modalities into clean conditions and noisy targets during training, enabling omni-directional conditional generation instead of fixed mappings. Decoupled Gated LoRA (DGL) introduces per-modality LoRAs that are activated when a modality serves as the generation target, preserving the strong priors of the VDM. Cross-Modal Self-Attention (CMSA) shares keys and values across modalities while keeping modality-specific queries, facilitating information exchange and inter-modal alignment. We instantiate UniVidX in two domains: UniVid-Intrinsic, for RGB videos and intrinsic maps including albedo, irradiance, and normal; and UniVid-Alpha, for blended RGB videos and their constituent RGBA layers. Experiments show that both models achieve performance competitive with state-of-the-art methods across distinct tasks and generalize robustly to in-the-wild scenarios, even when trained on fewer than 1,000 videos. Project page: https://houyuanchen111.github.io/UniVidX.github.io/

AI.tldr: UniVidX is a unified multimodal framework that utilizes video diffusion models for versatile video generation, overcoming limitations of training separate models for each task.
AI.motivation: To enhance video generation across various multimodal tasks without restricting input-output mappings and to model correlations across modalities effectively.
AI.method: UniVidX uses a shared multimodal space for conditional generation, featuring Stochastic Condition Masking for flexible condition settings, Decoupled Gated LoRA for preserving prior knowledge, and Cross-Modal Self-Attention for improved information exchange.
AI.result: Experimental validation shows UniVidX achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art methods in both intrinsic and blended RGB video generation, maintaining robustness with minimal training data.
AI.conclusion: UniVidX effectively enhances multimodal video generation, achieving high-quality results across multiple tasks while enabling flexibility and inter-modal coherence.

title: When LLMs Stop Following Steps: A Diagnostic Study of Procedural Execution in Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often achieve strong performance on reasoning benchmarks, but final-answer accuracy alone does not show whether they faithfully execute the procedure specified in a prompt. We study this question through a controlled diagnostic benchmark for procedural execution, where models are given a step-wise arithmetic algorithm and two numeric inputs, and must return the final computed value. The benchmark uses simple arithmetic operations but increases complexity through algorithm length and look-back dependencies over intermediate variables. Across 14 models and 55 datasets, average first-answer accuracy drops from 61% on 5-step procedures to 20% on 95-step procedures. Generation-level analysis shows that failures often involve missing answers, premature answers, self-correction after an initial error, under-executed traces, and hallucinated extra steps. These findings suggest that apparent reasoning ability can mask substantial weaknesses in faithful instruction execution.

AI.tldr: LLMs show declining accuracy in executing complex arithmetic procedures, revealing weaknesses in their instruction following despite apparent reasoning skills.
AI.motivation: The aim is to explore whether LLMs faithfully execute procedural instructions, rather than merely measuring their output accuracy.
AI.method: A controlled diagnostic benchmark was developed for evaluating procedural execution in LLMs, using step-wise arithmetic algorithms with varying complexity.
AI.result: The analysis reveals a significant drop in first-answer accuracy from 61% on simpler procedures to 20% on more complex ones, identifying specific failure modes in the execution process.
AI.conclusion: The study indicates that LLMs' apparent reasoning capabilities may conceal critical deficiencies in their ability to faithfully execute instructions.

title: Group Cognition Learning: Making Everything Better Through Governed Two-Stage Agents Collaboration
comment: This study has been Accepted by ICML 2026. The current version is a manuscript, please refer to the official version released at ICML 2026 for the final published version
summary: Centralized multimodal learning commonly compresses language, acoustic, and visual signals into a single fused representation for prediction. While effective, this paradigm suffers from two limitations: modality dominance, where optimization gravitates towards the path of least resistance, ignoring weaker but informative modalities, and spurious modality coupling, where models overfit to incidental cross-modal correlations. To address these, we propose Group Cognition Learning (GCL), a governed collaboration paradigm that applies a two-stage protocol after modality-specific encoding. In Stage 1 (Selective Interaction), a Routing Agent proposes directed interaction routes, and an Auditing Agent assigns sample-wise gates to emphasize exchanges that yield positive marginal predictive gain while suppressing redundant coupling. In Stage 2 (Consensus Formation), a Public-Factor Agent maintains an explicit shared factor, and an Aggregation Agent produces the final prediction through contribution-aware weighting while keeping each modality representation as a specialization channel. Extensive experiments on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and MIntRec demonstrate that GCL mitigates dominance and coupling, establishing state-of-the-art results across both regression and classification benchmarks. Analysis experiments further demonstrate the effectiveness of the design.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Group Cognition Learning (GCL), a method to improve multimodal learning by addressing modality dominance and spurious coupling through a two-stage protocol involving selective interaction and consensus formation, achieving state-of-the-art results in various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To improve the efficiency and accuracy of centralized multimodal learning by tackling issues of modality dominance and spurious modality coupling.
AI.method: Group Cognition Learning (GCL) employs a two-stage protocol with Selective Interaction for directed interactions and Consensus Formation for collaborative predictions, utilizing agents to manage these processes.
AI.result: GCL demonstrates significant improvements over existing methods, outperforming previous state-of-the-art results in regression and classification tasks on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and MIntRec datasets.
AI.conclusion: GCL effectively mitigates the challenges of modality dominance and spurious coupling, validated through extensive experimental results.

title: Advancing Edge Classification through High-Dimensional Causal Modeling of Node-Edge Interplay
summary: Edge classification, a crucial task for graph applications, remains relatively under-explored compared to link prediction. Current methods often overlook the potential causal influences of node features on edge features, leading to a loss of relevant prior information. In this work, we present an empirical exploration using the Causal Edge Classification Framework (CECF). Unlike conventional causal inference methods, CECF is the first framework to apply causal inference principles to the edge classification task and to explore modeling edge features as a high-dimensional treatment within a causal framework. Based on the node embedding of Graph Neural Network (GNN), CECF seeks to learn a balanced representation of high-dimensional edge features by mitigating the potential influence of node features. Then, a cross-attention network captures the complex dependencies between node and edge features for final edge classification.Extensive experiments demonstrate that CECF not only achieves superior performance but also serves as a flexible, plug-and-play enhancement for existing methods.We also provide empirical analyses, offering insights into when and how this high-dimensional causal modeling framework works for the edge classification.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Causal Edge Classification Framework (CECF) for edge classification in graphs, which incorporates causal inference principles to enhance performance by addressing node feature influences.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the under-explored area of edge classification and to improve upon traditional methods that ignore the causal relationships between node features and edge classifications.
AI.method: The paper presents CECF, which uses high-dimensional causal modeling to analyze edge features while leveraging node embeddings from Graph Neural Networks. It employs a cross-attention network to model complex dependencies.
AI.result: CECF demonstrates superior performance compared to conventional methods and is adaptable as an enhancement for existing edge classification approaches.
AI.conclusion: The empirical analyses indicate the effectiveness of CECF, providing insights into its applicability and benefits in the edge classification task.

title: InpaintSLat: Inpainting Structured 3D Latents via Initial Noise Optimization
summary: We present a training-free approach for controllable 3D inpainting based on initial noise optimization. In the structured 3D latent diffusion framework, we observe that the underlying geometric structure is established during the early stages of the diffusion process and exhibits high sensitivity to the initial noise. Such characteristics compromise stability in tasks like inpainting and editing, where the model must ensure strict alignment with the existing context while synthesizing a new structure. In this paper, we introduce a strategy to optimize the initial noise within the structured 3D latent diffusion framework, ensuring high-fidelity 3D inpainting. Specifically, we update the initial noise by leveraging a backpropagation approximation grounded in the rectified flow model, with the spectral parameterization specially designed for robust and efficient structured 3D latent optimization. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements in contextual consistency and prompt alignment over representative training-free inpainting baselines, establishing initial noise control as an independent dimension for 3D inpainting, orthogonal to conventional sampling trajectory manipulation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a training-free method for controllable 3D inpainting that optimizes initial noise in a 3D latent diffusion framework.
AI.motivation: To address instability in 3D inpainting and editing due to sensitivity to initial noise in the diffusion process, ensuring better context alignment while generating new structures.
AI.method: The strategy involves optimizing initial noise using a backpropagation approximation based on the rectified flow model, with spectral parameterization for enhanced robustness and efficiency in 3D latent optimization.
AI.result: Experiments show improved contextual consistency and prompt alignment compared to baseline methods in training-free inpainting.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing initial noise is identified as a key dimension for enhancing 3D inpainting performance, distinct from traditional methods of manipulating sampling trajectories.

title: ResRL: Boosting LLM Reasoning via Negative Sample Projection Residual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) enhances reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs) but usually exhibits limited generation diversity due to the over-incentivization of positive rewards. Although methods like Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) mitigate this issue by upweighting penalty from negative samples, they may suppress the semantic distributions shared between positive and negative responses. To boost reasoning ability without losing diversity, this paper proposes negative sample projection Residual Reinforcement Learning (ResRL) that decouples similar semantic distributions among positive and negative responses. We theoretically link Lazy Likelihood Displacement (LLD) to negative-positive head-gradient interference and derive a single-forward proxy that upper-bounds representation alignment to guide conservative advantage reweighting. ResRL then projects negative-token hidden representations onto an SVD-based low-rank positive subspace and uses projection residuals to modulate negative gradients, improving reasoning while preserving diversity and outperforming strong baselines on average across twelve benchmarks spanning Mathematics, Code, Agent Tasks, and Function Calling. Notably, ResRL surpasses NSR on mathematical reasoning by 9.4\% in Avg@16 and 7.0\% in Pass@128. Code is available at https://github.com/1229095296/ResRL.git.

AI.tldr: This paper presents Negative Sample Projection Residual Reinforcement Learning (ResRL) to enhance reasoning in Large Language Models while maintaining generation diversity.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve reasoning abilities in LLMs without compromising the diversity of generated outputs, addressing limitations in existing reinforcement learning methods.
AI.method: The paper proposes ResRL, which decouples semantic distributions of positive and negative responses, projects negative-token representations onto a low-rank positive subspace, and employs this projection to modulate negative gradients.
AI.result: ResRL shows improved reasoning and generation diversity, outperforming strong baseline methods, including Negative Sample Reinforcement, across various benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ResRL effectively enhances reasoning while preserving diversity, demonstrating superior performance in benchmarks related to Mathematics, Code, Agent Tasks, and Function Calling.

title: PILIR: Physics-Informed Local Implicit Representation
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks have become a powerful mesh-free method for solving partial differential equations, but their performance is often limited by spectral bias. Specifically, in standard MLPs used in PINNs, the global parameter coupling causes the model to prioritize learning low-frequency components, resulting in slow convergence for high-frequency details. To overcome this limitation, we introduce the Physics-Informed Local Implicit Representation (PILIR). Our approach separates the global physical domain into a discrete latent feature space and a continuous generative decoder. By using a learnable grid to encode explicit spatial locality, PILIR can capture high-frequency details locally, preventing dilution by global patterns. A generative neural operator then synthesizes these local latent features into continuous physical fields, allowing accurate reconstruction of fine-scale structures. Experiments on a range of challenging PDEs show that PILIR effectively mitigates spectral bias, thereby boosting the convergence of high-frequency details and achieving superior accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Physics-Informed Local Implicit Representation (PILIR) to address spectral bias in Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), improving convergence for high-frequency details in solving PDEs.
AI.motivation: The primary motivation is to overcome the spectral bias in standard MLPs used in PINNs, which hinder the learning of high-frequency components due to global parameter coupling.
AI.method: PILIR separates the global physical domain into a discrete latent feature space and a continuous generative decoder, employing a learnable grid to encode spatial locality, enabling local capture of high-frequency details.
AI.result: PILIR effectively mitigates spectral bias, enhancing the convergence of high-frequency details and demonstrating superior accuracy in experiments on various challenging PDEs compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach significantly improves the performance of PINNs by accurately reconstructing fine-scale structures in physical fields.

title: Prediction of Alzheimer's Disease Risk Factors from Retinal Images via Deep Learning: Development and Validation of Biologically Relevant Morphological Associations in the UK Biobank
comment: Accepted to the "Journal of Alzheimer's Disease" for publication
summary: The systemic, metabolic, lifestyle factors have established associations with Alzheimer's Disease (AD) through epidemiologic and AD-specific biomarker studies. Whether colored fundus photography (CFP) contains retinal structural signatures corresponding to these AD-related risk domains remains unclear. To determine whether deep learning (DL) models can predict 12 AD-related risk factors from CFP and to characterize the retinal structures underlying these predictions, thereby assessing whether CFP reflects pathways to AD vulnerability. Using UK Biobank CFPs, DL models were trained using 62,876 images from 44,501 unique participants to predict 12 factors linked to AD incidence: 6 categorical (sex, smoking, sleeplessness, economic status, alcohol use, depression) and 6 continuous (age, age at completing education, BMI, systolic, diastolic blood pressure, HbA1c). Model performance, model saliency, and saliency-derived scores (CAM-Score) were evaluated and compared to retinal morphometry. The scores were also compared between incident-AD cases (average 8.55 years before onset) and matched controls. Performance of DL ranged from AUROC= 0.5654-0.9480 for categorical and R2=-0.0291-0.7620 for continuous factors, outperforming most of the morphometry-machine learning models. Saliency-based score consistently highlighted biologically meaningful regions, particularly the optic nerve head and retinal vasculature. It also aligned with present morphometric variations. Several saliency-based scores differed significantly between incident AD and matched controls, suggesting potential overlap between retinal correlates of risk factors and preclinical AD-associated changes. CFP encodes retinal signatures linked to AD risk factors. Although not diagnostic, DL-derived retinal representations may uncover biologically meaningful risk-related structural changes mirroring the potential AD vulnerability.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the predictive capability of deep learning models using colored fundus photography (CFP) to identify Alzheimer's Disease (AD) risk factors.
AI.motivation: Understanding the link between retinal features and AD risk factors could provide a novel approach to early detection of Alzheimer's Disease.
AI.method: Utilizing 62,876 CFP images from 44,501 participants, deep learning models predicted 12 AD-related risk factors, comprised of 6 categorical and 6 continuous variables, while assessing model performance and the structural relevance of the predictions.
AI.result: Deep learning models achieved AUROC values between 0.5654 and 0.9480 for categorical factors and R2 values from -0.0291 to 0.7620 for continuous factors, outperforming most traditional machine learning methods based on retinal morphometry.
AI.conclusion: CFP can capture retinal structural signatures linked to AD risk factors, indicating that deep learning-derived images may reveal significant preclinical AD-related changes.

title: Towards Robust and Scalable Density-based Clustering via Graph Propagation
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2508.02989
summary: We present \textit{CluProp}, a novel framework that reimagines varied-density clustering in high-dimensional spaces as a label propagation process over neighborhood graphs. Our approach formally bridges the gap between density-based clustering and graph connectivity, leveraging efficient propagation mechanisms from network science to mitigate the parameter sensitivity inherent in traditional density-based methods. Specifically, we introduce a deterministic density-based propagation strategy to ensure scalable neighborhood identification. The framework is agnostic to the choice of distance metric and exhibits superior performance on large-scale data, processing millions of points in minutes while consistently outperforming existing baselines in accuracy.

AI.tldr: CluProp is a new framework for density-based clustering in high dimensions that uses label propagation over neighborhood graphs to improve performance and reduce parameter sensitivity.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of traditional density-based clustering methods, particularly their parameter sensitivity and inefficiency in high-dimensional spaces.
AI.method: The approach utilizes a label propagation process on neighborhood graphs and introduces a deterministic density-based propagation strategy for scalable neighborhood identification.
AI.result: CluProp processes millions of data points quickly and demonstrates superior accuracy compared to existing clustering methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively bridges density-based clustering and graph connectivity, making it a robust solution for large-scale clustering challenges.

title: DMDSC: A Dynamic-Margin Deep Simplex Classifier for Open-Set Recognition on Medical Image Datasets
summary:
Medical imaging datasets are often characterized by extreme class imbalances, where rare pathologies are significantly underrepresented compared to common conditions. This imbalance poses a dual challenge for Open-Set Recognition (OSR): models must maintain high classification accuracy on known classes while reliably rejecting unknown samples unseen during training in the clinical settings. While recently proposed Deep Simplex Classifier (DSC)~\cite{cevikalp2024reaching} and UnCertainty-aware Deep Simplex Classifier (UCDSC)~\cite{Aditya_2026_WACV} successfully leverage Neural Collapse to ensure maximal inter-class separation, they rely on a uniform margin that does not account for the varying densities of medical classes.
  In this paper, we propose DMDSC an enhanced framework featuring a dynamic margin approach. Our approach automatically adapts class-specific margins based on label frequency, enforcing a higher penalty and tighter feature clustering for rare pathologies to counteract the effects of data imbalance. Extensive experiments conducted on diverse medical benchmarks on BloodMNIST\cite{medmnistv2}, OCTMNIST\cite{medmnistv2}, DermaMNIST\cite{medmnistv2}, and BreaKHis~\cite{spanhol2015dataset} datasets, demonstrate that our framework outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: This paper presents DMDSC, a novel framework that introduces class-specific dynamic margins to improve Open-Set Recognition in medical imaging by addressing class imbalance issues.
AI.motivation: The study addresses extreme class imbalances in medical imaging datasets, which complicates Open-Set Recognition by necessitating accurate classification of known classes while effectively rejecting unknown samples.
AI.method: DMDSC employs a dynamic margin approach that adjusts class-specific margins based on label frequency, creating tighter clustering for rarer pathologies to mitigate the effects of data imbalance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple medical benchmark datasets (BloodMNIST, OCTMNIST, DermaMNIST, BreaKHis) demonstrate that DMDSC significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in Open-Set Recognition.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DMDSC framework effectively enhances the performance of models on imbalanced medical classification tasks by adapting margins according to class frequency, leading to improved recognition accuracy and robustness.

title: Foundation AI Models for Aerosol Optical Depth Estimation from PACE Satellite Data
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures, to appear in 2026 IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium
summary: Aerosol Optical Depth (AOD) retrieval is essential for Earth observation, supporting applications from air quality monitoring to climate studies. Conventional physics-based AOD retrieval methods formulate the problem as a pixel-wise inversion, relying on radiative transfer modeling, memory-intensive look-up tables, and auxiliary meteorological data. While recent data-driven approaches have shown promise, many fail to exploit the spatial-spectral coherence of hyperspectral imagery, leading to spatially inconsistent and noise-sensitive retrievals. We present the first study exploring Foundation AI models for AOD retrieval and propose ViTCG, a Vision Transformer with Channel-wise Grouping-based spatial regression framework that reduces retrieval bias and error. ViTCG uses hyperspectral top-of-atmosphere radiance as input and jointly models spatial context and spectral information. Validation with PACE radiance observations demonstrates a 62% reduction in mean squared error compared to state-of-the-art foundation models, including Prithvi, and produces spatially coherent AOD fields.

AI.tldr: ViTCG, a new Vision Transformer model, significantly improves AOD retrieval by utilizing hyperspectral imagery while ensuring spatial coherence, achieving a 62% reduction in error compared to existing models.
AI.motivation: To enhance the accuracy and reliability of Aerosol Optical Depth retrieval by leveraging Foundation AI models, addressing limitations of traditional and recent data-driven approaches.
AI.method: The proposed approach, ViTCG, employs a Vision Transformer framework with Channel-wise Grouping for spatial regression, integrating hyperspectral top-of-atmosphere radiance data to effectively combine spatial and spectral information.
AI.result: Validation against PACE radiance observations shows ViTCG achieves a 62% reduction in mean squared error compared to leading models like Prithvi and successfully generates spatially coherent AOD fields.
AI.conclusion: ViTCG represents a significant advancement in AOD retrieval, offering improved accuracy and coherence in atmospheric measurements through innovative modeling of hyperspectral data.

title: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Double Oracle Efficiency in Policy Optimization and Offline Estimation
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) in large environments often suffers from severe computational bottlenecks, as conventional regret minimization algorithms require repeated, costly calls to planning and statistical estimation oracles. While recent advances have explored offline oracle-efficient algorithms, their computational complexity typically scales with the cardinality of the state and action spaces, rendering them intractable for large-scale or continuous environments. In this paper, we address this fundamental limitation by studying offline oracle-efficient episodic RL through the lens of log-barrier and log-determinant regularization. Specifically, for tabular Markov Decision Processes (MDPs), we propose a novel algorithm that achieves the optimal $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ regret bound while requiring only $O(H\log\log T)$ calls to both the offline statistical estimation and planning oracles when $T$ is known and $O(H\log T)$ calls when $T$ is unknown. Crucially, this oracle complexity is entirely independent of the size of the state and action spaces. This strict independence drastically reduces the planning oracle complexity, representing a substantial improvement over existing offline oracle-efficient algorithms (Qian et al., 2024). Furthermore, we demonstrate the versatility of our framework by generalizing the algorithm to linear MDPs featuring infinite state spaces and arbitrary action spaces. We prove that this generalized approach successfully attains meaningful sub-linear regret. Consequently, our work yields the first doubly oracle-efficient (i.e., efficient with respect to both statistical estimation and policy optimization) regret minimization algorithm capable of solving MDPs with infinite state and action spaces, significantly expanding the boundaries of computationally tractable RL.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a novel offline oracle-efficient reinforcement learning algorithm for large environments, achieving optimal regret bounds with reduced computational complexity independent of state and action space sizes.
AI.motivation: To overcome computational bottlenecks in reinforcement learning caused by conventional regret minimization algorithms in large-scale or continuous environments.
AI.method: The authors propose a new algorithm for tabular Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) that uses log-barrier and log-determinant regularization, achieving optimal regret bounds while minimizing oracle calls.
AI.result: The algorithm reaches a regret bound of $	ilde{O}(	ext{sqrt}(T))$ with $O(H	ext{log log} T)$ and $O(H	ext{log} T)$ calls to oracles for known and unknown $T$, respectively, independent of state and action sizes.
AI.conclusion: This work introduces the first doubly oracle-efficient algorithm for MDPs with infinite state and action spaces, enhancing computational tractability in RL.

title: Static and Dynamic Graph Alignment Network for Temporal Video Grounding
summary: Temporal Video Grounding (TVG) aims to localize temporal moments in an untrimmed video that semantically correspond to given natural language queries. Recently, Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) have been widely adopted in TVG to model temporal relations among video clips and enhance contextual reasoning by constructing clip-level graphs. Despite their effectiveness, existing GCN-based TVG methods encounter three critical bottlenecks: 1) Most methods construct graph nodes using either static or dynamic features alone, resulting in incomplete visual representation and overlooking complementary semantics, 2) Most methods construct temporal graphs in a query-agnostic manner, leading to inefficient feature interaction within the temporal graph representation, and 3) Most methods often suffer from a single-granularity semantic matching, while direct training on complex temporal localization task may lead to slow convergence and suboptimal precision. To address these challenges, we propose Static and Dynamic Graph Alignment Network (SDGAN). First, SDGAN jointly exploits static and dynamic visual features to construct two complementary temporal graphs and performs Position-wise Nodes Alignment, enabling more expressive and robust visual representation. Second, SDGAN introduces Query-Clip Contrastive Learning and Adaptive Graph Modeling to explicitly align visual clips with their corresponding textual queries, yielding query-aware visual representations. Third, SDGAN incorporates multi-granularity temporal proposals within Progressive Easy-to-Hard Training Strategy, effectively bridging coarse-grained semantic localization and fine-grained temporal boundary refinement. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that SDGAN achieves superior performance across complex TVG scenarios. Codes and datasets are available at https://github.com/ZhanJieHu/SDGAN.

AI.tldr: SDGAN improves Temporal Video Grounding by addressing limitations in graph representation and training strategies.
AI.motivation: To tackle the limitations of existing GCN-based approaches in modeling temporal relations and enhancing semantic representation for temporal video grounding tasks.
AI.method: SDGAN utilizes both static and dynamic visual features to create complementary temporal graphs, employs Query-Clip Contrastive Learning to align visuals with text, and integrates multi-granularity proposals with a Progressive Easy-to-Hard Training Strategy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show SDGAN achieves superior performance on multiple benchmark datasets for complex temporal video grounding tasks.
AI.conclusion: SDGAN effectively addresses critical bottlenecks in existing methods and improves overall performance in TVG scenarios.

title: Mesh Field Theory: Port-Hamiltonian Formulation of Mesh-Based Physics
comment: 23 pages, 4 figures, 10 tables. Accepted to ICML 2026
summary: We present Mesh Field Theory (MeshFT) and its neural realization, MeshFT-Net: a structure-preserving framework for mesh-based continuum physics that cleanly separates the physics' topological structure from its metric structure. Imposing minimal physical principles (locality, permutation equivariance, orientation covariance, and energy balance/dissipation inequality), we prove a reduction theorem for mesh-based physics. Under these conditions, the physical dynamics admit a local factorization into a port-Hamiltonian form: the conservative interconnection is fixed uniquely by mesh topology, whereas metric effects enter only through constitutive relations and dissipation. This reduction clarifies what must be fixed and what should be learned, directly informing MeshFT-Net's design. Across evaluations on analytic and realistic datasets, physics-consistency tests, and out-of-distribution validation, MeshFT-Net achieves near-zero energy drift and strong physical fidelity (correct dispersion and momentum conservation) along with robust extrapolation and high data efficiency. By eliminating non-physical degrees of freedom and learning only metric-dependent structure, MeshFT provides a principled inductive bias for stable, faithful, and data-efficient learning-based physical simulation.

AI.tldr: Mesh Field Theory (MeshFT) and its neural implementation, MeshFT-Net, enable stable and efficient mesh-based continuum physics simulations by separating topological and metric properties while ensuring adherence to physical principles.
AI.motivation: The goal is to create a stable and efficient framework for mesh-based continuum physics simulations that uphold physical accuracy and learning efficiency, addressing issues observed in current methods.
AI.method: MeshFT involves establishing a theoretical reduction theorem that separates topology from metrics and employs a port-Hamiltonian form to maintain physical laws. MeshFT-Net operationalizes this theory in a neural network architecture.
AI.result: MeshFT-Net demonstrates near-zero energy drift and maintains strong physical fidelity across various datasets, performing well in terms of dispersion and momentum conservation, and showing robustness in extrapolation and data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The separation of topological and metric structures leads to a principled approach that enhances learning-based simulation accuracy and stability by focusing on essential metric-dependent features.

title: PhysEdit: Physically-Consistent Region-Aware Image Editing via Adaptive Spatio-Temporal Reasoning
summary: Image editing instructions are heterogeneous: a color swap, an object insertion, and a physical-action edit all demand different spatial coverage and different reasoning depth, yet existing reasoning-based editors apply a single fixed inference recipe to every instruction. We argue that adaptivity along both the spatial and temporal axes is the missing degree of freedom, and we present PhysEdit, an editing framework built around this principle. PhysEdit introduces two inference-time modules that compose without retraining the backbone. At its core, (1) Complexity-Adaptive Reasoning Depth (CARD) predicts edit complexity directly from the instruction and reference image and allocates the reasoning step count N_r and reasoning-token length r per sample -- turning a previously fixed inference schedule into a conditional-computation problem. CARD is supported by (2) a Spatial Reasoning Mask (SRM) that extracts an instruction-conditioned spatial prior from cross-attention to confine reasoning to regions that semantically require it. On the full 737-case ImgEdit Basic-Edit Suite, PhysEdit delivers a 1.18x wall-clock speedup (64.3s vs. 76.1s per sample) over a strong reasoning baseline while slightly improving instruction adherence (CLIP-T 0.2283 vs. 0.2266, +0.7%) and matching identity preservation within noise (CLIP-I 0.8246 vs. 0.8280). The speedup is category-dependent and reaches 1.52x on appearance-level edits, validating CARD's adaptive allocation as the principal source of efficiency gain. A 30-sample pilot with full ablations isolates the contribution of each module.

AI.tldr: PhysEdit enhances image editing by adapting reasoning complexity and spatial focus for diverse editing instructions, leading to faster and more accurate edits without retraining the model.
AI.motivation: Different image editing tasks require varying levels of reasoning and spatial attention, which existing frameworks do not adequately address.
AI.method: PhysEdit introduces two modules: Complexity-Adaptive Reasoning Depth (CARD) for dynamic reasoning steps based on task complexity, and Spatial Reasoning Mask (SRM) for localized attention during edits, both operating without retraining the original model.
AI.result: PhysEdit achieves a 1.18x speedup in processing time on the ImgEdit Basic-Edit Suite while improving instruction adherence and matching identity preservation, with efficiency gains peaking at 1.52x for appearance-level edits.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive framework effectively balances speed and accuracy in image editing tasks by intelligently allocating reasoning depth and spatial focus.

title: M-CaStLe: Uncovering Local Causal Structures in Multivariate Space-Time Gridded Data
comment: 19 pages and 6 figures in the main text; 33 pages and 11 figures total
summary:
Causal graph discovery for space-time systems is challenging in high-dimensional gridded data, which often has many more grid cells than temporal observations per cell. The Causal Space-Time Stencil Learning (CaStLe) meta-algorithm was developed to address that niche under space-time locality and stationarity assumptions, but it is currently limited to univariate analyses. In this work, we present M-CaStLe. M-CaStLe generalizes the local embedding and parent-identification phases of CaStLe to jointly model local within-variable and cross-variable space-time causal structures in gridded data. Like CaStLe, by constraining candidate parents to a constant-size space-time neighborhood and pooling spatial replicates, M-CaStLe increases effective sample size to make discovery tractable in high-dimensional settings. We further decompose the resulting multivariate stencil graph into reaction and spatial graphs to aid interpretation in complex settings.
  We study M-CaStLe in four settings: a multivariate space-time vector autoregression benchmark with known ground truth, an advective-diffusive-reaction partial differential equation verification problem with derived physical reference structure, an atmospheric chemistry case study in a low-temporal-sample regime, and an El Niño Southern Oscillation study on reanalysis data, identifying phase-dependent ocean--atmosphere coupling. Across these settings, M-CaStLe more accurately recovers multivariate causal structure in controlled settings and identifies important physical dynamics in real-world case studies. Overall, M-CaStLe advances causal discovery for multivariate space-time systems while retaining interpretability at the grid level.

AI.tldr: M-CaStLe enhances causal graph discovery in high-dimensional space-time systems by extending the capabilities of CaStLe for multivariate analyses, enabling the modeling of local and cross-variable causal relationships.
AI.motivation: To improve causal discovery methodologies for high-dimensional gridded data, addressing the limitations of CaStLe which only supports univariate analyses.
AI.method: M-CaStLe generalizes CaStLe by jointly modeling both within-variable and cross-variable causal structures, utilizing a local space-time neighborhood to enhance effective sample size and facilitate causal discovery.
AI.result: M-CaStLe demonstrates improved accuracy in recovering multivariate causal structures across varied benchmarks and case studies, successfully identifying crucial physical relationships in real-world data.
AI.conclusion: M-CaStLe represents a significant advancement in multivariate causal discovery for space-time systems, providing robust interpretation capabilities at the grid resolution.

title: Learning Coarse-to-Fine Osteoarthritis Representations under Noisy Hierarchical Labels
summary: Knee osteoarthritis (OA) assessment involves a natural but often underused label hierarchy: a coarse binary OA decision and a fine-grained Kellgren--Lawrence (KL) severity grade. Existing deep learning studies commonly treat these targets as separate classification problems, either reducing OA assessment to disease presence or directly optimizing noisy ordinal KL labels. In this work, we ask whether this clinical hierarchy can serve as a representation-level supervisory prior. Rather than introducing a complex architecture, we use a deliberately simple dual-head model with a shared encoder and two task-specific heads as a probe of hierarchical supervision. We compare single-OA, single-KL, and dual-head training across multiple 3D backbones under the same test protocol. Beyond standard classification metrics, we perform paired statistical comparisons, analyze latent severity-axis geometry, and examine saliency overlap with cartilage regions. The results show that dual-head supervision produces backbone-dependent gains, with clear improvements in KL-related metrics for selected backbones. More importantly, the gains are accompanied by a more ordered coarse-to-fine latent organization and, for responsive backbones, stronger anatomical alignment of saliency with cartilage. These findings suggest that even simple hierarchical dual-head supervision can reshape disease representations under noisy coarse/fine labels, providing a useful inductive bias for OA diagnosis and severity grading.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the efficacy of hierarchical supervision in knee osteoarthritis assessment using a simple dual-head deep learning model, finding that it enhances disease representation and improves performance metrics.
AI.motivation: To explore if the clinical hierarchy in knee osteoarthritis assessment can be utilized for improving the classification of disease presence and severity.
AI.method: A dual-head model with a shared encoder was employed, testing single-task and dual-task training across various 3D backbones, and performance was evaluated using classification metrics and statistical analyses.
AI.result: Dual-head supervision led to backbone-specific improvements in KL metrics and an ordered latent organization, with enhanced anatomical alignment of saliency in certain models.
AI.conclusion: Implementing a simple hierarchical dual-head supervision can effectively enhance disease representations in osteoarthritis, aiding in diagnosis and severity assessment.

title: Unpaired Image Deraining Using Reward-Guided Self-Reinforcement Strategy
summary: Unsupervised deraining has attracted attention for its ability to learn the real-world distribution of rain without paired supervision. However, the lack of strong constraints makes it difficult for the network to converge, especially with the complex diversity of rain degradation. A key motivation is that high-quality deraining results occasionally emerge during training, which can be leveraged to guide the optimization process. To overcome these challenges, we introduce RGSUD (Reward-Guided Self-Reinforcement Unsupervised Image Deraining), comprising two key stages: reward recycling and self-reinforcement (SR) training. For the former stage, we propose an Image Quality Assessment (IQA)-based dynamic reward recycling mechanism that selects optimal derained outputs during training and continuously collects high-quality deraining images. In latter stage, we incorporate these rewards into the model's optimization process, constraining the optimization space and improving alignment between derained outputs and clean images. By leveraging IQA-based self-reinforced loss and dynamically updated rewards, we enhance the quality of synthesized pseudo-paired data and stabilize the optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves SOTA performance across multiple datasets, including paired synthetic, paired real, and unpaired real images, outperforming existing unsupervised deraining approaches in both subjective and objective IQA metrics. Additionally, we show that the self-reinforcement strategy is adaptable to other unsupervised deraining methods and our deraining framework demonstrates strong generalization across existing supervised deraining networks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RGSUD, a method for unsupervised image deraining that utilizes a reward-guided framework to improve model convergence and output quality.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the observation that high-quality deraining outputs can occur during training, which can guide the optimization process despite challenges in converging due to diverse rain degradation.
AI.method: RGSUD consists of two stages: reward recycling, which uses an IQA-based dynamic mechanism to select and gather optimal derained outputs, and self-reinforcement training that integrates these rewards into the optimization process to improve alignment with clean images.
AI.result: RGSUD achieves state-of-the-art performance on various datasets, outperforming existing unsupervised deraining methods in both subjective and objective IQA metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed self-reinforcement strategy enhances not only its own deraining framework but is also adaptable to other unsupervised methods, demonstrating strong generalization across supervised networks.

title: Rethinking LLM Ensembling from the Perspective of Mixture Models
summary: Model ensembling is a well-established technique for improving the performance of machine learning models. Conventionally, this involves averaging the output distributions of multiple models and selecting the most probable label. This idea has been naturally extended to large language models (LLMs), yielding improved performance but incurring substantial computational cost. This inefficiency stems from directly applying conventional ensemble implementation to LLMs, which require a separate forward pass for each model to explicitly compute the ensemble distribution. In this paper, we propose the Mixture-model-like Ensemble (ME). By reinterpreting the ensemble as a mixture model, ME stochastically selects a single model at each step to generate the next token, thereby avoiding the need to explicitly compute the full ensemble distribution. ME is mathematically equivalent to sampling from the ensemble distribution, but requires invoking only one model, making it 1.78x-2.68x faster than conventional ensemble. Furthermore, this perspective connects LLM ensembling and token-level routing methods, suggesting that LLM ensembling is a special case of routing methods. Our findings open new avenues for efficient LLM ensembling and motivate further exploration of token-level routing strategies for LLMs. Our code is available at https://github.com/jialefu/Mixture-model-like-Ensemble/.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Mixture-model-like Ensemble (ME) for efficiently ensembling large language models (LLMs) by sampling from one model instead of computing the full ensemble distribution.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiency of conventional ensemble methods applied to LLMs that require multiple forward passes, resulting in high computational costs.
AI.method: The paper proposes a method that reinterprets LLM ensembling as a mixture model, allowing stochastic selection of a single model at each step to generate the next token instead of calculating the entire ensemble distribution.
AI.result: ME achieves 1.78x-2.68x speedup compared to conventional ensemble methods while maintaining mathematical equivalence to sampling from the ensemble distribution.
AI.conclusion: The ME approach not only enhances the efficiency of LLM ensembling but also connects it to token-level routing methods, encouraging further research in these areas.

title: BWLA: Breaking the Barrier of W1AX Post-Training Quantization for LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have driven major progress in NLP, yet their substantial memory and compute demands still hinder practical deployment. Binarization can compress weights to 1 bit, fundamentally lowering compute and bandwidth cost. However, existing methods cannot address activation heavy tails and thus must keep activations in high precision, preventing true end-to-end acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose BWLA (Binarized Weights and Low-bit Activations), the first post-training quantization framework that preserves high accuracy while achieving 1-bit weight quantization together with low-bit activations (e.g., 6 bits). The Orthogonal-Kronecker Transformation (OKT) learns an orthogonal mapping via EM minimization, converting unimodal weights into symmetric bimodal forms while suppressing activation tails and incoherence. The Proximal SVD Projection (PSP) then performs lightweight low-rank refinement through proximal SVD projection, further enhancing quantizability with minimal overhead. On Qwen3-32B, BWLA reaches a Wikitext2 perplexity of 11.92 under 6-bit activations (vs. 38 from SOTA), improves five zero-shot tasks by more than 70%, and delivers 3.26 times inference speedup, demonstrating strong potential for real-world LLM compression and acceleration.

AI.tldr: BWLA is a novel post-training quantization framework for LLMs that achieves 1-bit weight quantization and low-bit activations, significantly improving efficiency while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address the impractical memory and compute demands of LLMs, enabling their deployment in real-world applications.
AI.method: BWLA combines 1-bit weight quantization with low-bit (e.g., 6-bit) activation through the Orthogonal-Kronecker Transformation for weight mapping and Proximal SVD Projection for low-rank refinement.
AI.result: BWLA achieves a perplexity of 11.92 on Wikitext2 with 6-bit activations, outperforming state-of-the-art methods and enhancing five zero-shot tasks by over 70%, while providing a 3.26 times speedup in inference.
AI.conclusion: The framework shows significant promise for compressing and accelerating large language models, making them more practical for deployment.

title: Exploring the Limits of End-to-End Feature-Affinity Propagation for Single-Point Supervised Infrared Small Target Detection
summary:
Single-point supervised infrared small target detection (IRSTD) drastically reduces dense annotation costs. Current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods achieve high precision by recovering mask supervision through explicit, offline pseudo-label construction, such as multi-stage active learning and physics-driven mask generation. In this paper, we study a minimalist alternative: generating point-to-mask supervision online through in-batch, point-anchored feature-affinity propagation. We instantiate this paradigm as GSACP, an end-to-end testbed that directly supervises the detector using hard-margin feature affinity gated by local image priors, entirely eliminating external label-evolution loops.
  This compact design, however, exposes an optimization bottleneck. Because the affinity target is generated from the same feature representation being optimized, training forms a self-referential loop. We theoretically formalize this as \emph{Self-Referential Propagation Drift}, a representation-supervision entanglement that can sharpen true boundaries or distort the feature space to satisfy its own targets. To systematically isolate these failure modes, we apply a protocolized single-variable ablation procedure spanning local EMA teacher decoupling, hard-background contrastive separation, and adaptive support geometry.
  On the SIRST3 dataset, GSACP-Final establishes a new ultra-low false-alarm operating regime, achieving a highly competitive $0.6674$ mIoU while demonstrating a $38\% relative reduction in false-positive artifacts ($\mathrm{Fa}$) compared with PAL. By systematically deconstructing the end-to-end paradigm, we map its performance boundaries and show that in-batch feature propagation provides a compact alternative for deployment scenarios where false-alarm suppression is paramount.

AI.tldr: The paper presents GSACP, a minimalist approach to infrared small target detection (IRSTD), which generates point-to-mask supervision online, overcoming optimization challenges associated with self-referential loops while achieving competitive results on the SIRST3 dataset.
AI.motivation: Current SOTA methods for IRSTD depend heavily on elaborate offline pseudo-labeling techniques, increasing annotation costs, necessitating a more efficient method.
